author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody:
People. This is a maple-ginger "custard" pie, with tofu and cashew cream making up the custard base. No one is going to come to your house and force you to eat the ginger tofu pie. So if you find yourself at a roadblock because the recipe for the ginger tofu pie contains ginger and tofu, and you don't like ginger and can't eat tofu...maybe this is not the recipe for you. 

(Feel free to experiment on your own with ginger-less, tofu-less, cashew-less variations and report back!)

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody:
When I make this pie, I will peel, cube and steam the orange sweet potatoes. It will only need 15mn or so of steaming.

Having made cashew cream before, I will stress that a very powerful food processor or blender is critical to achieve a very smooth batter. Otherwise there is an unpleasant "graininess" that remains.

Thank you for what looks to be a delicious vegan dessert... the kind where nobody needs to know it's vegan because it's just delicious (except for the vegan eaters, of course).

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: I had the same thought, but I wonder if it's steamed whole to reduce moisture. In that case, why not roast? I'm curious!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody:
I agree with Leanna that cubed sweet potatoes may result in too much moisture. If you cube, I suggest spreading them out on a sheet pan and letting them dry out a little before proceeding. 

I would definitely not roast at the risk of dryness or caramelization. Cheers.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: Thank you! This looks fantastic and I am excited to make it :) I truly appreciate all the wonderful vegan/plant-based recipes from NYT. As a long-time plant-based chef and cooking instructor, I am impressed! Please keep them coming _/\_

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: If you soak the cashews in warm/hot water, it can cut down your soaking time and they will blend easier.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: How far in advance of the big day can this be prepared?

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: I made this yesterday and just tasted it today (along with my 4 year old who shockingly also liked it). It is really delicious!!! I am not a vegan and this recipe does not taste like a vegan recipe. The texture is spot on— it really fools you to think it is a cheesecake! I used a purple Japanese sweet potato, which made it a rich dark purple color that I quite enjoy. It does not taste like sweet potato at all— I assume the sweet potato merely contributes to texture? I added extra powdered ginger to the filling because I didn’t have the maple extract on hand so wanted to amp the ginger flavor (I plan to buy maple extract before making this on Thanksgiving). I also subbed about a third of the brown sugar with maple sugar to try to amp the maple flavor that way. I did a cheat with the cashews— instead of soaking for a few hours, I microwaved them in a bowl of water for 1-2 minutes. Since I cut the recipe in half to test it out, I couldn’t get the maple syrup and cashews to fully purée in the food processor (there wasn’t enough volume). I ended up adding the tofu to the cashew and maple syrup mixture, which provided enough volume to completely purée it. Despite halting the recipe and baking it in a 7 inch springform pan, I found that it needed the same bake time specified in the recipe. If you have family members who can’t eat eggs or dairy, this is a wonderful holiday option!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: The recipe calls for silken tofu but doesn’t specify soft, medium or firm/x-firm. ???

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody:
@Jen silken is a soft desert tofu —I’m pretty sure it’s always a similar consistency no matter the brand or manufacturer. Unless I’m
Misunderstanding the question?

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: @Jen silken tofu is a very soft tofu

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: @Jen silken tofu IS a specific type of tofu in terms of texture lol

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: I just finished making this with Japanese purple sweet potatoes and can confirm that the color is beautiful! A really rich medium purple color. Now on to cooling to see how it tastes!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: @Annie The suspense is killing me.!How was it?

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: As this is a Thanksgiving suggestion, it would be so nice to know how far in advance we can make the components, and how long will it nicely keep?? Thank you! Sounds delicious.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: If you really want to get as much water as possible out of the silken tofu, drain it, then scoop it out into a ceramic bowl and microwave it for about thirty seconds. This will release even more liquid. Drain just by holding hand over side of bowl and pour excess water into sink.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: Could graham crackers be used for the crust instead of ginger snaps? Could purple Japanese sweet potatoes be used instead of regular Japanese sweet potatoes? I have some from my CSA and thought it would be colorful!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: @Jessica Jenen I too was considering using purple sweet potatoes from my CSA! I’m curious if it ends up being more lavender than vibrant purple.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: @Annie I made sweet potato pie with Japanese purple sweet potatoes and it was a medium lavender color after baking.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: My husband is allergic to cashews. What would you suggest as a subsitute?

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: @edepayre raw skinned almonds would work if other tree nuts are okay. Just do t include the skins because that would give a grainy texture and make the mixture darker. I bet macadamia nuts could also work. I’d avoid walnuts or pecans because the skins could mess it up

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody:
Can I leave out the Maple extract if I don’t have it? Or what can I use as a substitute?
Thanks

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody:
@Bea
I use coconut sugar syrup which has less calories but is not quite as nutritious as honey.  If you can’t buy the syrup - you make it with coconut sugar and water: heat to boiling point then quickly turn down to a simmer until syrup thickens.  Allow to cool!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: Why not use maple syrup?  My plan is to sub out some of the brown sugar with syrup (or you can use maple sugar).

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: @Bea I’m wondering if using maple sugar would help amp up the maple flavor…

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody:
Yum! The ginger and maple are a great flavor combo and I didn’t even use the maple extract. The little saltiness in the crust added a really nice depth. I used a vitamix to puree for the most silky texture, and it did not disappoint! 
  Best part is telling an unsuspecting omni that they just ate tofu pie!!!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: It's great, I made it. Very, very sweet, between the cookie crust, the pie filling and the topping; I will probably see where I can cut down on sugar even a little bit next time I make it. But overall delish!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: I LOVE this recipe. I made it for our Thanksgiving dessert, and it was a hit with the omnivores. Truly delicious, and easy to make. I already had baked Japanese sweet potatoes in the fridge, so I used them (instead of steaming). Thank you for another wonderful vegan recipe - please keep them coming! _/\_

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: Perfect!  I sped things up by microwaving the cashews in water and microwaving the sweet potato.  Next time I might add cocoa powder because chocolate.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: This dessert was a last minute addition to our Thanksgiving day menu, and it was everything I hoped it would be! Once I skimmed through the recipe, I couldn’t not make it because it sounded like Thanksgiving perfection. Indeed it had a very nice balance of flavors, and was an item that appealed to everyone in attendance, not just the vegans. I used boiling water for quicker cashew soak time. Beware if using a Vitamix as mentioned in earlier comments: while the Vitamix works well for making the custard filling, this can be a terrible way to make the cookie crumb crust, which I learned upon liquifying it. Easily resolved since I have a Cuisinart food processor and had purchased extra ginger snaps. The upside was I baked both crusts, and the messed up one became a lovely cookie crumble topping to eat with ice cream (or “frozen nondairy coconut dessert”), and to add a little extra crunch once the pie leftovers became a bit soggy.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious! My guests loved it and no one suspected it was vegan. I substituted vegan butter for the coconut oil and I added some ground cinnamon to the topping. I found the crust to be a bit too salty and will use less next time. I thought the filling could have used a little more sweetening so I think next time I'll drizzle some maple-syrup drizzle on top.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: This pie is so delicious, no one needs to know it is vegan. The second time I made it I halved the flour in the topping and I liked that better.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: Is there a recommended substitute for the cashews if you're making this for someone with nut allergies?

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: @ENE I think that is a really hard one because the nuts provide richness and thickening power here. I might honestly seek out a different recipe— perhaps one that uses vegan cream cheese.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: Is cashew cream available at the market? could one substitute cashew butter mixed with almond milk or some such? BTW IMO cashew butter the kind in the dairy case makes fab mashed potatoes.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: I can’t find Maple Extract. Would the recipe work without it? Any substitutes you would suggest?

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: Why do vegan recipes always use coconut oil? Just curious. I hate coconut anything and I am adept enough to sub, so just wondering???

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: @Mary it works well texturally because it is solid at room temperature, which helps thicken/stabilize in the way saturated fat from dairy works. If you hate the flavor, just don’t buy raw coconut oil. Get the “refined” version of coconut oil, which is flavorless.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: Can I use roasted cashews instead of raw?

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: Wondering if canned pumpkin could replace the sweet potato from those who have already made this?

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: Moisture might be a little higher in the pumpkin so lightly roasting the pumpkin first may help to give a similar consistency to the sweet potato

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: Can I make this 2 days before I intend to serve it?

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: Test drove this one on Thanksgiving. It was a  delicious alternative to the more traditional pumpkin, pecan, and apple pies. The crust was  the real star. The contrast between the crust, the custard, and the pecan topping was perfect.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: Before I just go on and on about how much I loved this, I should have made the crust a little thinner & a little higher than the precise inch because I wound up with extra filling. I love to bake traditional flour-and-butter-filled pastries, but this is the kind of thing I actually love to eat. Oh my goodness, it was fantastic. The saltiness… the unapologetic gingeriness. I’m slightly sad that I sent the few leftovers home with my friend who is gluten and dairy free. I want a whole one all to myself.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: I don’t have a springform pan. Is there any other bakeware that would work?

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: Vegan ginger snaps?

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: @Margie - I use a classic, family gingersnap recipe that has been handed down. It has no egg. I substitute a high quality vegan alternative for the butter.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: In a pinch you can also buy biscoff biscuits (which are vegan) and add some powdered ginger and other spices

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: @Margie I actually was surprised that a lot of the grocery varieties were vegan!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: I certainly want to try this for deliciousness--I'm lucky in having no food sensitivities. For family Thanksgiving, though, the one beloved niece who can't do gluten or dairy can't do soy, either.   Any suggestions for a substitute?

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: @Judith that’s a really tricky one because the tofu really contributes to the texture and volume of the final product. I might try an entirely different recipe if you need it to be soy free. There are vegan cheesecake recipes out there that just use cashews and no tofu that may work better, as opposed to substituting something else for the tofu.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: @Judith you could try Pumfu—pumpkin seed tofu. I’ve seen it at Natural Grocers. Not sure it’d be the same texturally but I’d bet your niece would be so touched by the attempt. Google Byranna’s vegan pumpkin pie. I made it yearly and my SIL asked her mom to stop making the Libby’s recipe and has me make two pies. Different recipe but it’s sooooo good

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: @Jessie Oh no, Pumfu is good, but the texture, flavor, and color would be terrible in this context

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: Could you substitute cashew butter for the soaked cashews?

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: @Katelyniffereny different texture. You could probably water down the cashew butter to make cream but it might still be grainier than doing it with soaked cashews

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: Can you substitute sunflower seeds for the cashews? My granddaughter is allergic to tree nuts but I would love to make this.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: My vegan family hates ginger and can't eat all that coconut oil. What can I substitute?

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody:
@vacciniumovatum

Raw Cacao; mango or coconut condensed milk? Raspberries?

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: I’m thinking Biscoff cookies.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: @vacciniumovatum is the coconut oil an allergy? If not, it’s only 5 tablespoons of coconut oil, total in the full recipe. If you hate the flavor of coconut oil, just use refined coconut oil, which is flavorless.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: @S in VA immersion blender works best for cashews in my experience.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: @ Jenn: Silken tofu is the term for soft tofu

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: @Ania Silken tofu is not the same as soft tofu. Silken tofu can be soft, medium and firm. For more on tofu types, go here: https://www.seriouseats.com/shopping-cooking-guide-different-tofu-types

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: I wonder if chestnuts can sub for cashews: we have family member with a cashew allergy, and this year i have my first chestnut harvest!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: @judielaine I don’t think so— chestnuts are very low fat and mostly carbs, so they would work differently from cashews, which are fat rich and help mimic the texture of heavy cream/cream cheese.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: I love vegan recipes. However a severe allergy to cashews especially just always makes me wonder why I’m so special lol!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: Any alternatives to pecans someone has tried? I’m just not a fan of pecans or walnuts in desserts.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: Why does it take 11 hours to make? Soaking the cashews seems like the longest piece

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: @SYStrauss, the 11 hours includes the overnight in the fridge.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: @Mks roasting makes the cashews drier/raw cashews add more creaminess through added moisture. there is also thr flavour difference- raw are bland aka neutral, roasted are the opposite. Substitute at your own risk

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: can the pie be frozen?

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: I haven't cooked with coconut oil before, and I see there are solid varieties and also oil that is already liquid. Any preference? It would seem easier to just buy the liquid version, but I'm wondering if texture etc. are different.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: You want to look for unrefined coconut oil. More flavor and minimal processing. This is generally solid at room temp this time of year. You can zap it in the microwave after spooning it out into a glass dish and it'll be liquid no problem.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: I made this and thought it was excellent. I used the Mori-Nu brand of silken tofu (extra firm). I actually omitted the topping and instead toasted some pecans, chopped them, tossed with a small amount of turbinado and granulated sugar and scattered it in a circular pattern around the top after the pie was fully chilled.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: This came out great! Can I freeze portions to enjoy later?

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: @Emily I’m wondering the same thing…

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody:
Average score from my thanksgiving table (of both vegans and meat-eaters) was 8.7/10!
Delicious fantastic pie I really love it.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody:
A great addition to Thanksgiving. Salty, sweet, creamy, gingery. Tasted like a professional dessert.
It was a lot of work doing it the first time. I made gluten and dairy-free ginger snaps. I appreciated the suggestion of making sure the cashews are blended till creamy. The flavors and textures are very well balanced. Yum!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: I made this today and it was delicious!! I cut down on the brown sugar and maple syrup by half in the filling because I prefer things not too sweet. I would make this again but I think instead of adding ginger to the filling I would keep the salted maple and add some vanilla. Definitely use a Japanese sweet potato as it has a firmer texture. Soak cashews in boiling water for faster processing.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: This turned out great! My dairy-free, gluten-free kiddo loved it! I made the filling the night before and it worked just fine. The crust turned out a bit crumbly but that might be because I made it in a fluted tart pan. Overall amazing recipe. Might try adapting it with pumpkin or other flavors!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody:
Excellent dessert! Mine looked just like the photo (except for a small crack, which I’m not sure if a water bath might help next time). I added about 1-2 tsp of extra chopped ginger and forgot to buy the maple extract. My Whole Foods sells Japanese sweet potatoes. I also bought their 365 brand silken tofu and ginger snaps. 

The pie had a real zing to it, perfect for ginger lovers. It was creamy but not heavy. The saltiness of the crust was quite complementary. I think it would be impossible for a non-vegan to know this has tofu in it (and my family agrees), but it’s definitely not trying to pass itself off as cheesecake form a texture perspective. 

Commenters are wondering how far in advance you could make this. Since the recipe says to refrigerate preferably overnight, I’d say it would be just fine to make and serve this two days in advance.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: wow!  this came out wonderful!  crust was a little crumbly on the sides but that was my own error in construction.  nice and creamy, great texture and flavor all around. let it set up in the fridge overnight/24hrs.  it was a hit for our vegetarian/vegan thanksgiving!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: This pie was a big hit for vegans and omnivores alike.  I followed this recipe exactly except I used cashew powder instead of whole nuts which allows one to skip the soaking and also means a food processor is not required for that step.  I am not a skilled baker and while this recipe has many steps and ingredients it is not technically difficult

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody:
I subbed in walnuts for the pecans in both the crust and topping (needed to save my pecans for pecan pie) and it was still so delicious! I think I may have used the wrong type of Japanese sweet potato because mine was significantly more brown. I dotted and swirled in some persimmon jam to the custard to add color and fruitiness- delicious!

I have a kitchenaid food processor. I soaked the cashews in hot water for 1 hour, and took plenty of time to blend them. It seemed like it wasn’t going to get totally smooth, but with patience eventually I did achieve that silky texture that I was worried only my vitamin would achieve.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: I needed to run the cashew/tofu mixture through a blender to get a creamy texture and I accidentally threw out the maple extract I had ordered, so added vanilla instead. Other than that I made it as written and it was very delicious! Would never know it was vegan.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody:
Brought this pie over to my daughter's for Thanksgiving with her meat-and-potatoes in-laws. I brought only one piece home, so guess it was a hit! I reduced the brown sugar by half for both the custard and the crust as everything is too sweet for me, and it worked out fine. I often make tofu pudding with cocoa and stevia and today it was tofu (yes, firm is fine), stevia and ginger with added ginger juice from the Ginger People.  Yum!
Thanks for all the work you put into this recipe, Tanya!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: Definitely a bit weird. The ginger was overpowering.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: This was a hit at our Thanksgiving table. I added the appropriate spices (cinnamon, gloves, allspice, nutmeg, and more ginger) and everyone said it tasted a lot like pumpkin pie. I also subbed butter for the coconut oil as no one in our group was a strict vegan.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: I made this yesterday and it is amazing! I substituted a narrow yellow sweet potato (aka a kumara for any New Zealanders out there) because I couldn't find a Japanese sweet potato. Also skipped the maple extract, but still had a nice maple flavor from the maple syrup. Everyone loved it!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: This pie is pretty involved, but it has a big payoff. I made this for Thanksgiving and everyone loved it. Notwithstanding the caveat about ginger tolerance, our crowd were all ginger lovers, and next time I make it, I'll add a teaspoon or so of ground ginger at the end of blending step 6. The crust and topping are perfect.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: Great pie with a delicious flavor! I made one version as written, and adapted another for a family member who couldn't have soy - subbed out the tofu for four blended dates, an extra sweet potato and a can of coconut cream, and only added about a tbsp of brown sugar. Both turned out really well, and the soy free one firmed up perfectly! Very adaptable, delicious pie. Adding it to my regular holiday rotation!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: The crust ended up too salty for my tastes, but since I’m not actually vegan, I am eating the leftovers with unsweetened whipped cream, which cuts the salt perfectly. I love the strong ginger and the texture is very satisfying.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: I did the same with vegan whipped cream, unsweetened @MountainZ

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody:
So, I used the white Japanese sweet potato and light maple syrup and mine came out an odd grayish, brown color.  Also used GF ginger snaps which next time will add a little Oat flour to help with binding.  The crust was super crumbly.   

Hubby and I adored it.  For other guests, it was either love or hate based on strong ginger flavor.  

I made unsweetened whipped cream which for me and two other guests was perfect to off set the salty-sweetness.  Hubby and others preferred as is.  

No one could tell it was tofu or vegan.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: This took me hours the night before Thanksgiving and it was so disappointing.  My family politely ate it but I found it inedible as it was so overly sweet.  Really quite disgusting.  I threw it out the next day.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: I wanted to love this recipe but it just didn’t turn out for me. While the crust was fantastic (will use again as a sub in other recipes), I found the filling to be out of balance flavor-wise. Perhaps I didn’t add enough maple, but it tasted almost medicinally gingery. I also could not get the filling to a silky smooth consistency despite soaking my cashews overnight and steaming my sweet potato for longer than directed. The texture wasn’t bad, just seemed off.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: Made as described in recipe but did use vitamix for cashew cream. The salt was necessary and everyone thought it was delicious. I’ll definitely make it again.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: Can this pie be frozen?

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: I made this for Thanksgiving and my 12-year-old nephew said this was the best pie he'd ever had.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: Can this pie be successfully frozen?

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: Can this be made a day ahead of serving it?

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody:
I didn't like this cake as much as i would have liked to, though aspects of it were amazing. I loved the silken tofu and steamed sweet potato but I don't agree it needed fresh ginger per se, iI would have gone with a bit less and I think powder would have been fine. Perhaps less maple syrup also...or something. 

My curiosity is piqued however, with this silken tofu stuff, amazing! And I will try the vegan chocolate mousse next.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
recipeTitle: Vegan Salted Maple Ginger Pie Recipe
commentBody: I made this for our library cooking club and it got a lot of complements.  As with any first-time recipe, there were a lot of steps, but it is not a complicated recipe. I thought it might have too much coconut flavor, but there was none in the chilled pie. I did think this would do better in an 8" springform pan as there was a smidgeon too little crust for the 9" version.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Parchment paper tip: WET the parchment paper, wring it out, blot it dry. It will now be malleable and can easily be molded to fit your pan. No need to cut several pieces or use multiple layers.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Just crumple the paper in its new state. It becomes very malleable after. No need to wet

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody:
I have the original recipe from La Vina restaurant in San Sebastian that was used as the inspiration for this recipe. No idea where I got it from but it uses a 6" pan (I use a Fat Daddio) and it's amazing. It's also super quick to make. It fits in our toaster oven.

The big difference I noticed was that this recipe doesn't specify letting it sit in the fridge overnight, which helps to solidify the cake.

I also make a quick graham cracker crust for it, as it sometimes leaks out the bottom.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: @David G. Do you mean resting it in the refrigerator before or after it’s baked?

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: @Stacy A it would be after it’s baked to cool and serve.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: @David G. We tried it room temp and cooled. Cooled in fridge overnight took it up a notch ( as if that were possible!)

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody:
Been doing these cheesecake for years and its much better if you undercook it, around 30 min in the oven and then keep it in the fridge overnight for perfect texture.
Real deal like in La Viña de San Sebastián :)

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: @Ricard I followed this advice and the middle of my cheesecake was all goopy even after 24 hours in the fridge :(

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: @Amanda that's how it's supposed to be https://www.facebook.com/reel/1422321268779217?mibextid=9drbnH&s=yWDuG2&fs=e

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: @Ricard I don’t remember getting a chilled piece at la vinia, supposed to be room temperature and very very soft on the inside. Consistnaxybis not supposed to be NY style.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody:
Re. Resizing the recipe for an 8” pan, here are quantities that should work, converted to easy measurements:
1 1/4 cups sugar
3 2/3 packs cream cheese
Scant 1/4 tsp salt
4 large eggs
1 1/2 cups heavy cream
22 1/2 Tbsp flour
Hope this helps!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: @Sally Ann I think you meant 2 1/2 Tablespoons and not 22, right?

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody:
@Sally Ann Thank you for these measurements! Just to make it even easier, 3 2/3 packs (8 oz. each) of cream cheese equals 2 8 oz. packs of cream cheese. 
Here’s the math: 3 x 2 = 6. 6 / 3 = 2.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: @GMT It 3 and 2/3 packs.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: This was bomb but I note that it makes 24 servings?! What kind of a magician can get a slice that thin out of a room-temp cheesecake?! What I’m saying is that I had a ~750-calorie slice of this cake and it was worth every bite.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Another trick to make parchment paper pliable is to wrinkle it up into a tight ball so that it has lots of creases. It's equivalent to wetting and wringing it out, without having to dry it off.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I had this cake three years ago in San Sebastián, and I am still trying to burn off the calories. It contains huge amounts of everything I'm not supposed to eat, and I don't care. This one really is worth dying for.   Make it for guests, and they'll thank you the rest of their (shortened) lives!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I made this for the first time. The pleated parchment paper was better in theory than practice. Thanks to others for the tips offered here. I took the cheesecake to a potluck and the platter looked like it had been scavenged by hyenas.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I  have made this numerous times just as written. My only suggestion is to bake on a cooling rack inside a baking sheet pan. The direct heat has a way of burning the bottom.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: In Northern Spain, I discovered that many cooks add a little blue cheese to the recipe. It tastes amazing!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: @Nicholas Boncardo totally agree : I hesitated first to go with the Gorgonzola but it brings another dimension… sweet with a zesty salty test - the umami cheesecake- you have to try it.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Nothing better than this delight with a portion of cooked peppered strawberries and a dollop of creme fraiche.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Cooked peppered strawberries...?!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: @LindaN pepper on strawberries is delicious!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: @Gus that’s gilding the lily

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody:
Excellent cheesecake! I successfully made it gluten free by using 1/4 cornstarch instead of flour.

On my second attempt to make this cake  I added the juice of 1/2 lemon and zest of whole lemon. The results were fantastic.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: @Tal re the added lemon juice-- the extra liquid didn't affect the cook time or consistency?

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: @Tal I used a gluten free flour and it worked perfectly as well. I think at these proportions the flour only serves as a binder. I’d bet tapioca starch would work too…

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: This is a divine dessert that is uncomplicated to make.Pls add vanilla! I follow the bon appétit recipe, since it calls for entire packages of cream cheese and heavy cream to be used, unlike this recipe. It's 2 pounds cheese, 2C heavy creme, 6 eggs, 1.5C sugar, 1/4C flour. Easy to halve the recipe. It tastes better chilled in refrigerator overnight.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I don't think the recipes are that different .. The Bon Appetite recipe uses less cheese, 1 extra egg, a little extra sugar. They both specify 2 cups of heavy cream.  I imagine they come out pretty much the same.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Should it be refrigerated after making?

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Doesn't have to be. We just cover it like a regular cake.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: For those asking for a smaller recipe, this one from King Arthur is wonderful. Have made it numerous times. https://pin.it/3P8mB2K

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: @Nancy Miles  thank you! the KA version is not only smaller but gluten free / Passover friendly.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Why are some ingredients shown in ounces and grams and others only in ounces? Very frustrating for those of us in countries advanced enough to use the metric system.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: @Daryl 100%! Like stick to one system please - it’s so cumbersome

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: @Daryl I use the NYT Cooking app on my phone and there I can choose to see all measurements in metric system and temperature in Celsius, super useful.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Made several of these earlier this year, one with the flour and one without and both were delicious, for those of you looking for a gluten free version! If you have an instant read thermometer, baking to an internal temp of 185ºF (or actually pulling it out of the oven just below 185ºF) will yield a perfectly creamy delicious basque cheesecake. Was excellent with a lightly cooked cherry syrup!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody:
Made this yesterday. Absolutely delicious! Shared it with neighbors who swooned over it. A little runny in the middle possibly because I only had a 9" pan so it might have been a little thicker. Went the full 60 minutes. Maybe could have done another 5 but was nervous about it getting too dark (it's hard to see through my oven glass). 
One note for NYTimes staff: please be consistent with measuring units. Ounces or grams, not both.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: This cheesecake was amazing. Make it! It's so so easy. I placed the springform pan on a rimmed sheet just in case. I served it with fresh whipped cream and raspberry sauce made by melting raspberry preserves, straining out the seeds and mixing in fresh raspberries. Make sure to have lots of people to enjoy this otherwise there is high probability you will eat the entire cake.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody:
I’ve made one before and it didn’t include flour and it came out fine.  I’m following a gluten free diet and this is one of the few treats I bake that I can eat.
I’ve made it in a 9 inch and 6 inch pans, too,

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: @Lorraine I’m gluten free as well. So you didn’t substitute the flour with GF flour, no flour at all? It still works? Thanks in advance!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: @Stacy A I have made this several times, always with a GF blend (Bob’s, KAF,  NYT Cooking recipe), with great success each time.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: @Stacy A I’ve substituted coconut shavings for the flour. It was delicious.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Added 2 tablespoons vanilla, and refrigerated overnight. Turned out really great.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Has anyone made a smaller version of this in an 8 inch pan?   Wondering how to reduce ingredients.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: here is a recipe resizer: https://www.webstaurantstore.com/recipe_resizer.html

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Reduce each ingredient by 20%. So you need 80% of each one. Easy with this recipe, since you start with 5 eggs.  A scale makes this much easier for the other ingredients. A calculator helps too!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: An 8 inch pan is 64/100ths the size of a 10 inch, so reduce by a third.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I made the batter as described but put it into a 12 muffin pan (30 mins) and a smaller 8x10in glass pan (45m) no parchment, just a heavy coat of cooking spray. It works.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Just because there aren't enough calories in this as is, I like to make it with a biscoff cookie crust. I made this for thanksgiving, christmas, and it's a new favorite of family and friends. My sister said it's the best cheesecake she's ever had. My dad called it outrageous. People that don't even like cheesecake love it. I'm getting more requests for it than I can handle. This one is a decadent winner.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: @Lindsey D. Biscoff! How delicious!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: @Lindsey D. Did you cook the Biscoff crust before you added the cheesecake batter?

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: On lining cake pans with parchment paper: Zoe Nathan (Huckleberry) has a technique that works well for me. Fold a sheet of paper in half, then in half again on the other axis. Then fold the closed short edge to touch the closed long edge (like a paper airplane) and repeat. Crease the pleats, cut the ragged open edges straight across. When opened, it looks like a pleated cone and tucks neatly into a round pan- no wetting or crumpling required.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I have been making this cake for years.  It always comes out perfect.  My recipe has 7 eggs instead of 5 and the oven is 425. I add 2 tsp vanilla and the zest from 2 lemons.  I have also served it with some cajeta and ginger caramel sauce. (OMG) It is a great cake to have in your repertoire.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I wish this wasn't called "Basque cheesecake". It's delicious but it's not a Basque dish, it's the invention of one chef in San Sebastian. I live about 30min from San Sebastian and no-one in my village has ever heard of it.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I stopped using a spring form pan for making cheesecakes. I have a few straight sided 2.5 inch high pans of various sizes. When the cake first comes out of the oven I run a knife around the rim to make sure nothing is sticking, (although using parchment would eliminate this need.) After the cake cools completely I giggle it a little to make sure it's not sticking and I am able to carefully invert it and it comes out of the pan nicely. No way to prevent leakage with a springform absent a crust.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Giggle vs. jiggle! I have to say, the vision of someone giggling at their cake to make sure it is not sticking was utterly delightful!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody:
@Liz
I made a small investment in a new springform pan (Amazon) recommended by David Lebovitz that works perfectly for this cake and others. It has a glass base and a high silicone rim that makes a tight seal with the base. It has an ingenious bar that holds the rim together. When done, simply detach the bar and the rim easily separates from the cake, and it remains on the glass base for serving.  Nary a leak as well as a perfect-looking cake.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Absolutely wonderful. The only change I made was to add 1 tsp of vanilla. I listened to the commenter who said to bake it on a rack placed on a cookie sheet, and there was no burned bottom and no leaking. I also refrigerated it overnight and brought to room temp before serving. It was a big hit at Thanksgiving. No one even added the fruit topping I made, it was so delicious plain.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I'm wondering about the refrigeration - did yours cut and serve cleanly?  I did not, and mine was a disasterous -though delicious! - mess.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Austin’s Clark’s Oyster Bar served a version of this they called Basque Cake, if I recall correctly. They topped it with cherries that had a similar satisfying texture to Luxardos, and weren’t too sweet, syrupy or goopy.  Anyone who can convince the fine folks at Clark’s to share their cherry secrets will earn massive culinary karma.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody:
Been making this for years. Def need to let it first cool at room temp, then, the fridge for it to "set" I always have a Gram Crust as it adds another dimension. 
Last one I did was with Choc Chips (Winning!) Next with Strawberry Preserves in the center as it sets.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: There’s no need to wet the parchment paper. Simply press it down into a buttered pan and then butter the parchment paper. This dessert was an absolute hit with all my guests. After baking I left it out at room temperature to cool. This desert can stand on its own and does not require an accompaniment. Serve the desert at room temperature with a strong cup of coffee. The recipe is perfect.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I have found that the easiest way to deal with the parchment paper in these types of situations is to cut the parchment paper to the size needed and then scrunch it into a very tight ball.  It can easily be spread out to fit any type of pan.  Works like a charm!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: This turns out perfectly every time. I add 1 teaspoon vanilla. I make it gluten-free by using Bob’s Redmill one to one baking flour as a substitute for the wheat based flour.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Cheesecakes are so forgiving--don't overthink this recipe. I sent my husband to the store for 3 12-oz pkgs. of cream cheese, and he came home w/4 8-oz. pkgs. Of course, that's how they come, so I added a big dollop of sour cream already in the frig to make up the difference. I have an aversion to using parchment paper, so I buttered and sugared my spring form pan, then lightly whipped the cream before adding it to the rest of the ingredients. Worked fine--no leaks!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Seems to call out for some vanilla? Otherwise it’s delicious and wonderfully straightforward.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Hubby’s favorite and he hates cheesecake. Tip for effortlessly lining the pan with parchment: crumple a parchment sheet into a tight ball then smooth it out flat. It becomes pliable like fabric. A trick I learned at King Arthur Baking School. Crisscross two sheets for the pan for full coverage.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I have made this recipe, sometimes with gluten-free flour and sometimes without, many times and it always gets rave reviews. My problem with it is how to get the slices out of the pan and onto a plate without bringing the bottom layer of parchment paper with it. Any suggestions?

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Use a triangular pie server.  Make 3 slices as if you're serving 2 slices at the same time.  This makes it easier to pull away the first slice ( and successive slices) without the parchment paper.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: This is very similar to our family cheesecake recipe, "Machisimo Cheesecake" (funny story there).  We do a VERY thin crust (1mm or even less if we can pull it off!) Then 15 minutes at 500, then an hour at 350.  So ridiculously good!  And yes, breakfast for the next day, preferably with FRESH fruit ON THE SIDE. No goopy stuff on the top to ruin it!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody:
1 c. sugar 
3 lower-fat cream cheese and 1 package goat cheese
1 c. sour cream (instead of 2 c. heavy cream) 
Perfect for me.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I made the mistake of following other commenters advice and only cooked for 40 minutes, then refrigerated 24 hours. The middle was still all goopy. Next time I’ll follow the recipe as written and go for 60 minutes. Taste is amazing! Crumpling up the parchment paper and then folding out worked great.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: After divorcing my first wife, I found her non baking family recipe for cheese cake using both sweet cream and sour  cream a stable item.  In schools and family contacts, it became my routine.  This offering is different because it uses more cheese and eggs....but it is appreciated because it joins a universe of recipes that I might consider

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I crumple the dry parchment into a ball, unfold it & fit it into the pan.  I use clothes pins to hold the parchment in place until I fill it.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody:
Instead of heavy cream, I use créme fraîche or sour cream to add a slightly tangy, nutty taste. I use 15% less sugar than this recipe calls for. 

For a 15 cm/6-inch cake, measurements are: 420g cream cheese, 210g crème fraîche, 180g superfine sugar, pinch of salt, 3 eggs, 20 ml plain flour. 230C/450F convection oven for 35-45 minutes. Refrigeration optional. Adding vanilla would be gilding the lily.

Stir all eggs together then add to batter all at once to prevent over-beating.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I made this in a 9 inch springform and it got great reviews. The recipe is forgiving. It was still great even though I over baked it at about an hour and fifteen minutes. I kept leaving it in the oven longer thinking it should be less jiggly, but now I know it’s fine to take it out while it’s still VERY jiggly. I had to turn the oven up to 425 for the last ten minutes to get the top to brown. I’ll definitely make this again, and still turn the oven up for the last ten minutes. Even in the 9” pan, I think I’ll take it out after a 60-65 minutes. I did not miss the crust, nor did I feel like it needed any vanilla as some have suggested. I did serve with a quick strawberry compote by special request from my spouse, but it didn’t even need that either.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: This is the most amazing dessert. We tried the Basque cheesecake when we were in San Sebastian in 2023 and this recipe is spot-on. This is one of my go-to desserts especially when making a Spanish-themed dinner. The hardest part about this recipe is scraping the creme cheese out of the packages. It comes out perfect every time. I have made it many times since 2023. Everyone says this is the best cheescake they have ever tasted.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I made this! It is super creamy! I omitted flour (my daughter is gluten intolerant), 4 oz less of the cream cheese, and added 1/2 tablespoon vanilla extract. Cooked an hour--could have gone a little longer, but I was scared to take any chances. It's good!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Added 1t vanilla for flavor.  Baked it in a silicone-ringed springform pan with a glass base — no greasing or parchment needed — on a cooling rack in a sheet pan. Came out beautifully after cooling and served it right off the base. One guest: “This is the best cheesecake I ever had in my life.”  Mine, too.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody:
I was looking for a cheesecake using brie, and decided this would work. I substituted half of the cream cheese with brie (minus the rind) and well..
It's gone. Disappeared like magic and my guests raved.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I used two 9" cake pans lined with parchment paper instead of a springform pan. It was plenty of batter for two pans and it rose to the top of the pans during baking exactly as expected. I slightly reduced the baking time and gently shifted pans around during baking trying to get even baking and even browning.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I made this with 1 8oz packet of cream cheese roughly quartering the rest of the ingredients. I used a 6 inch cake tin and it turned out beautifully.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody:
*inadvertently used 32oz instead of 36oz cream cheese because supermarket math is hard
*used 9 inch springform, proofed up above the top and only spilled a tiny bit over during the last 2-3min of baking…sheet pan underneath worked great for catching (plus you can eat the fallen cheesecake morsels while you wait for the whole thing to cool!)
*ended up needing about 60m for 9in.
*VERY jiggly all over (not just center) but cooled beautifully
*endless compliments from everyone who tried it (family, coworkers). Everyone assumed it was super complex to make and was very impressed 
*served with fresh raspberries

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: @acl replying to my own comment…32oz cream cheese worked just fine and everyone loved it! Debating whether or not to try the “full” 36oz for thanksgiving!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Made this for my women's group and even with the initial comments of 'it looks burnt', it was gone in record time....and these are women who are very cautious about eating anything rich or caloric that might affect their waistlines! I love to tempt them with NYT recipes!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody:
Baked for 70 mins at 400° and was perfect. The center - but not the whole cake - was still jiggly and the top was caramelized, almost burnt. Would 100% make this again...and again.
..

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Used a 9” pan. Used the full recipe and I put in a ring of parchment paper that went 2-3” above the rim. The cake rose to the top of it then fell as expected.  None spilled out. Cooked 60 minutes. Turned out perfectly. It’s SO much better the next day after it forms up in the fridge.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: @Chris I echo your addition of the 2-3 inch parchment rim above the pan’s edge.  Happy to hear yours was a success.  I am slightly concerned that my finished cheesecake isn’t as brown/burnt as the videos with the recipe.  But hopefully a couple of days in the fridge will ensure it is solid enough for the crowd to enjoy it. 🙏🏻

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: @Chris After having a feeling I should actually measure and not eyeball my springform’s size seeing the huge amount of batter in my bowl… it was only 9” indeed, not 10”! God bless the NYT comments. Your method of adding a ring of parchment paper worked flawlessly. THANK YOU!!!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: How do I adjust the ingredients for a 9” springform pan?  Or is it better to not use all of the batter? Thank you!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I've cooked this a couple times...and even bought a 10" springform to make the recipe the second time. While an overnight in the fridge would help set the cake, eating it still a bit warm from the oven is heavenly. As a topping, I've been cooking strawberries in sugar/orange juice/butter with 50 cranks of black pepper. The black pepper/cream cheese chiaroscuro is divine.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody:
Used 9” springform pan
Baked 60 min, then broil 2 min to darken—perfect.
Cool 3 hrs, then overnight in fridge. 

Served with Amarena cherries. (24 oz jar pitted sour cherries   3/4 cup sugar, splash alerol, lemon juice). Chill a bit to thicken up

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I don't know.  I made this and it came out beautiful.  But the flavor seemed one-note boring and it did not have a "velvety, custardy interior."  Rather, it was a touch grainy in the way that cheesecakes sometimes are. It's possible I made an error.  Was it done at 50 minutes, though I kept it in the full 60?  My cream was a little colder than my other ingredients. Did that cause this?  It almost certainly wasn't the 6 oz of mascarpone I substituted because I didn't have a full 36 oz of Philly.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: @David B. I wonder if you beat the cream cheese and sugar long enough - that might explain the grainy texture. I beat them for a good while because I was worried about the sugar’s graininess, and the cheesecake turned out very light and custardy.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: i made this with 1/2 goats cheese and 1/2 cream cheese because its impossible to find real soft spanish cheeae in the US and it came out SPECTACULAR!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I used 20 oz of goat cheese and 16 oz of cream cheese. Texture is wonderful and tastes amazing.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Easily the best cheesecake I’ve ever had, but be warned, it’s incredibly moreish! A friend made this recipe the first time I tried it, and I kept thinking about it for days. I’ve personally baked it twice, it’s a very easy recipe to master!Definitely recommended to let it sit in the fridge overnight after baking, otherwise it doesn’t have the best texture (but honestly, I’ve eaten it just after baking when it was slightly warm, because I’m impatient, and it still tasted amazing, was just kind of fluffy and soft).

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I used chestnut flour (same measurement as the regular flour) because it was all I had that was gluten free and it turned out great. Added 1 tsp of vanilla as well.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: If you love cheesecake you have to try this recipe.  The batter comes together quickly and the result is wonderful.  I got rave reviews, with some saying it was the best cheesecake they have ever eaten.  I do agree with other commenters; you should let it rest in the fridge overnight, if possible, the result is the creamiest most dedicant cheesecake I have ever had.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Parchment paper tip: WET the parchment paper, wring it out, blot it dry. It will now be malleable and can easily be molded to fit your pan. OR just wrinkle it up and then put it in.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Can you substitute half and half for the heavy cream? I have an overabundance of the former.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Replying to my own comment to report that the substitution worked just fine and the cheesecake got rave reviews. I was the lone person in my group who was underwhelmed by it; we ate it several hours after it came out of the oven, cooled, and briefly chilled in the fridge. However, I just had some again, 24 hours after eating it the first time, and it is so, so good. So I am definitely in the 24 Hours Later Camp.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Make sure to use parchment paper IOT avoid any leakage of the runny batter from the pan.  Tastes even better after cooling in the refrigerator overnight.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I omitted the cream by accident the second time I made it!   It was less heavy.  Everyone loved it.   I will make a note of this for the next time I make it.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody:
I subbed in some goat's cream cheese, added a hint of vanilla and lemon zest.
I also made just under half the recipe, and cooked it in a loaf tin. worked brilliantly for thin slices for dessert after a big dinner

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Lemon zest and vanilla because I like those flavors in my cheesecake. Easy simple and delicious

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: @Danielle how much lemon zest?

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody:
I crumpled the parchment, used 2 large pieces that overlapped. Used avo oil to grease pan and parchment. 
Used 9 inch pan and did not alter recipe. 
Kept it one shelf lower than middle rack. 400 deg, total bake time over 50 minutes and it did not burn. Rotated it a couple of times. Nice dark brown top and sides. Middle was extremely jiggly. 
Don't need the tray under, no drips. 
Refrigerated overnight, perfect consistency. 

I forgot the lemon juice....

Served with plain sliced strawberries.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody:
5 packages of cream cheese
Sift flour on to medium plate so you can pour it in the mixer

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Holy Hannah, this is the simplest and most incredibly delicious cheesecake I’ve ever had.  Wow.  Simple, easy, and addictive.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody:
Unlike a heavy cheesecake, this recipe makes a light fluffy but delicious cake.  I did not want to make such a large cake so followed (more or less) the resizing seen in previous review.
I did use a 10 inch springform pan prepped as original recipe.
resized to make less of a dessert:
3--8 oz cream cheese
1  1/4 c sugar
pinch of salt--1/8 tsp
4 eggs
6 oz heavy cream
and baked at 400 for 45 min.. watching closely for desired browning.
Easy and quick to prepare.  wonderful cheese cake.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I made a Basque cheesecake last week, based on a recipe given to me by a friend in London (from the London Times). It requires three sheets of parchment paper, overhanging the pan. The proportions and ingredients are slightly different, e.g. it uses double cream, which I found at Zabar's. (Double cream is richer -- 48% fat -- than our heavy whipping cream); vanilla; and slightly less flour. I made the lemon variation, which uses lemon zest and lemon juice. It was fabulous!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I made and served this cake for dessert last week. Beautiful soft set consistency. Came together quickly in the food processor. I reduced the sugar, and added lemon zest and vanilla. Served with blueberry sauce made that day. Delicious. Have already shared the recipe with four friends.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody:
HEAVEN ON A FORK. This recipe is perfect; it should be a top recipe on the site! I’ve had variations and the wonderful thing abt basque cake is you can’t really mess it up. I divided this recipe as written into *two* 9” spring-form pans. Your dinner guests will devour both, promise!

Some notes: whipped cream cheese helps; add the scraped seeds from a good vanilla bean— the flavor adds nuance and the subtle flecks are beautiful; yes you should sift the flour so it doesn’t clump. TOPA!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody:
November 2024
For diabetics: make with 1 cup of Erythritol (whiz in blender to powdered consistency). Served with orange slices sautéed in Cointreau and decorated with zest of orange. Quite fabulous.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: So good. Made it home for the holidays in the suburbs aka the land of no spring form pans.  Substituted a parchment lined 9x9 Pyrex square and it still turned out perfect (but with some batter wasted).

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Sally Ann’s resize for 8 inch pan must have a mistake at the end as 22- 1/2 Tbs can’t be correct. Must likely meant 2-1/2 Tbs flour down from 1/4 cup for a 10 inch.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I would only remove 1 tablespoon flour from the original 1/4 cup for an 8" springform cake pan.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody:
33 oz reg blk cream cheese
2 oz St Agur Bleu
1 oz tub cream cheese

wet the parchment,scrunched, blotted 
baked 50min -  was very dark on top
baked the day before taking to Erika's - Matt took,I was sick.
Everyone loved it. I tried 3 days later and it was really good. Would be fine to add a little more blue, it gave a complexity, but no real blue flavour.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I made this cheesecake exactly one year ago for my partner's birthday. Even letting it cool in the pan for several hours, when I removed it from the springform, it lost its shape and melted for lack of a better term. I come back now to say - a good oven makes a difference, as well as a sifter! It turned out perfectly this year, even beating it by hand.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: This is a showstopper! I’ve made dozens of cheesecakes and am partial to Craig Claiborne’s New York style Cheesecake BUT! You must give this method a try—follow directions and do not substitute if possible, and you will be rewarded with most creamy, decadent, deeply delicious cheesecake. While it doesn’t require any embellishment (the almost burnt marshmallowy top is amazing) you could top it with some fresh summery fruit compote but why change a good thing!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I would recommend adding a generous blob of vanilla bean pasta (measured with your heart) into the mixture just to add a bit more flavour! It was delicious 😋

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Made this for a garden party, added fresh strawberries on the side. I didn’t fold the parchment paper - too challenging. It didn’t cook in the time suggested - might be my oven - took 20 minutes longer than noted.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I made this for my husband and sons when they came home yesterday from a camping trip that I wasn’t able to go on. My older son and husband are iffy about cheesecake. They LOVED this. I own a low-key organic and gf pies and cakes company in Maine and so I make my own gf mix. For this I used 1/8 cup tapioca starch and 1/8 cup potato starch and 1/2 tsp xanthan gum. It browned beautifully and I had enough to make a small cake on the side for my neighbors. I added vanilla ( good, long soupçon ) and served with fresh raspberry sauce with berries,l  I’d just picked, sugar and vanilla. Pretty off the charts!!!!! I’m embarrassed to say we ate the entire cake ( my two sons, husband and I, in two days). Make this cake! Don’t delay! It’s also splendidly easy!!!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Easy to make and boy was this was a crowd pleaser! I did as one of the reviewers suggested and refrigerated overnight and brought to room temp before serving. I had a build your own cheesecake bar with raspberry coulis, chocolate ganache and fresh raspberries. All 3 worked out just fine! My friend who usually brings the dessert oohed and aahed over it. 10/10

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: This turned out perfectly at 60 minutes.  I buttered the pan, and also th e parchment paper, and that helped it slide out from under the cake.  It is very rich, but seems light at the same time.  My only suggestion, because I love citris, would be to perhaps add a little lemon oil.  The hardest part of this cake is preparing the pan and opening the cream cheese boxes!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Family loved it with the macerated strawberries!  I have yet to make a bad recipe on the app… thank you!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: This recipe was ridiculously easy and straightforward. The cheesecake came out beautifully and it will be my go-to from now on. Delicious, easy and a showstopper! A trifecta!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: WOW - this is so delicious, light, not too sweet, creamy. My guests LOVED it and we're enjoying the leftovers. It was better the first day.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Reduced the sugar to 320 grams

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Try subbing some goat cheese in place of some of the cream cheese for a little tang- really good !

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: If youre European: cut the sugar by 50-80g. True for all American dessert recipes.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: This cheesecake is divine and a huge crown pleaser, even for ‘non-cheesecake’ people. I never reduce the recipe for my smaller springform, I make 1 or 2 smaller cooks samplers! Nice to taste before bringing someplace.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: This dessert is so easy and absolutely delicious. Parchment paper does not need to be wet just press it down into your 10 inch baking pan. I’d butter the pan first and then pressed the parchment paper into the pan and then buttered the parchment paper.Don’t change a thing with the recipe because it’s perfect and just like a crème brûlée you don’t need anything else to serve with it. I baked it the day before the party and kept it out at room temperature until it was served .

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Made just as instructed and it was perfect!  Made it the day before serving, and let it set at room temperature.  Splendid indulgence.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Since it was so recent Cat, would you mind sharing whether you found it eggy? I made a similar recipe recently and it was a little too eggy for my liking but this has a bit more cream cheese and 1 less egg so I'm hoping that will fix that!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Question: has anyone tried this with half & half?  (Feels like sacrilege tbh). I’m lactose-intolerant and can get LF half & half but have never found LF heavy cream.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I think one measurement of success is to be able to easily make a recipe when all the necessary “tools” are not available and there are few out of the ordinary ingredients needed. This is just such a recipe! While staying in a VRBO my son made this for my birthday and it was fantastic! Easy to do without a spring form pans using only a regular 9in cake pan with parchment paper lining, and easy to cook in an “iffy” oven. I would also agree, it’s even better the next day, slightly chilled.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: 36 ounces of cream cheese? My faulty math equated this to four blocks ( I like Cabot’s), which of course came up 4 ounces short. I compensated with some feta cheese which was in my fridge, reduced the amount of salt in the original recipe by a smidge, and the result was a triumph! Fantastic recipe!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I made this with quantities to fit in an 8" square pan and mixed in food processor (thanks, Cook Republic!). No parchment paper, no greasing the pan (lazy). Cooked c. 32 min. in my convection toaster oven. Came out perfect, dark on top. Dreamy like a cheesecake mousse while hot from the oven. More solid after refrigerating. Next time, shorter cook time for more gooeyness. Will try buttermilk in place of cream.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I serve this with a berry compote.  I slice strawberries/and or rasberries, put a pinch of sugar and a splash of a good quality balsamic vinegar. Put in the fridge for a couple of hours.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody:
2 lb cream cheese
2 c heavy cream
6 eggs
1.5 c sugar

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody:
I am not necessarily a cheesecake person.  What drew me to this recipe is the minimal ingredients and no crust.  Also the carmelization of the top, sides and bottom make a quazi crust to die for.

Nutritionally, it's not too sweet and junky.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody:
I made this exactly by the recipe in the late morning, leaving it at room temperature until time to serve that evening.  While it tasted amazing, it was a hot mess to cut and serve.  I used multiple pieces of parchment, and could not get the serving utensil in between the cake and the paper.  
1) If I had refrigerated it first, would it have served better?
2) Has anyone made a bottom crust to make serving easier?

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Yes, your mistake was to not refrigerate it.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Can a blender substitute for a stand mixer?

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody:
@MLS- I used my Cuisinart immersion blender, both the whisk and the blender blades.

Blender first then the whisk as I blended the sugar and cream cheese and then used the whisk for the eggs & the rest in a separate bowl and the whisk to combine all ingredients

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I followed the recipe exactly and this came out almost as perfect as the photograph.  I would not change a thing about this recipe.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Love this recipe! So easy and such a crowd pleaser. I add vanilla and substitute 8 oz of goat cheese for an equivalent portion of the cream cheese. Never tried the blue cheese, but will try that soon!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: It’s an amazing, decadent recipe. I made it as instructed and it turned out beautifully.  A Spanish friend who knows cheese cake said it was one of the best she’d ever had.  A little slice goes a long way and it freezes well.  Don’t get too fussed about the parchment - scrunch it in there as best you can and the weight of the batter should do the rest.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I made this last night after reading the tantalizing comments.  Even without a springform pan and adding too much heavy cream, this is a beautiful recipe. I’m looking forward to making a more savory version with the blue cheese.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: OK, I baked it and managed not to eat any, yet (it looks glorious). Several notes say it should be put in the fridge overnight - but covered or not? What is the best way to store it? Fridge, counter? I guess for now I'll loosely cover it with wax paper, sort of a compromise, covered but not too tight.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Had high hopes for this but it’s much less dense than traditional NY cheesecake, more “eggy”, like a cross between flan and cheesecake.  Very good, but not for me.  Will stick with traditional NY cheesecake recipe.  Yes, I know it may be comparing apples to oranges…

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I made the recipe exactly as described, but added a touch of orange extract just to give a hint of citrus. It elevated the flavor without overtaking. Once you've eaten basque cheesecake, you will never want to go back to the old standard.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody:
I add the a zest and juice from 1 naval orange
I also substitute-
2 bars of creamed cheese (8 oz ea)
1 small container whole milk ricotta (8 oz)
1 small container mascarpone (8 oz)
And 1/4 cup gf flour mix
This is our family favorite in the cheesecake realm!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: @sue- love this salmon!! But that’s not the same recipe!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody:
“The best dessert I’ve had since moving to Alaska!”
Creamy, light, smooth texture- outstanding with tart fresh raspberries   It rose over the top of my 9 inch pan- would use taller parchment paper next time to hold the crown.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: @AK baker I accidentally bought a 9” springform pan instead of 10”, so I took this advice to make the parchment a decent bit taller than the pan and it was great advice!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Does it absolutely need the flour? We have a GF family member. I have GF flour but it has a taste difference.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: The only change I made was adding a splash of vanilla. Cooked at 400 for 30 minutes, dropped to 375 for the rest of the cook time. Took it out around 45 minutes, tested the middle to make sure it’s over 185F. Set in fridge overnight. Was a hit for sure!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Unbelievable flavor. Absolutely fabulous and easy. I steeped the cream over night with pandan leaves. Thanks for that tip. Large dessert and everyone swooned over it.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I’ve now made three of these cheesecakes after a visit to San Sebastián in September where we ate the real thing at La Viña.  This is a very close replica! I’ve noted that I must be doing it right when the smoke alarms go off in the house! Everyone loved the cheesecake at Thanksgiving, and I am hopeful the crowd at Christmas this week will also rave about it.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody:
Great cheesecake.  Much lighter than I thought it would be.  I added zest of one lemon and the juice of half.  Next time I would add the juice of the whole lemon.  I served with a blueberry compote.
I made this at altitude (6000 ft), and I riased the temp to 415 and baked for 35 minutes.  The bottom of the center was not quite set, so next time I would bake at 400 and go longer.  The top was browning nicely and didn't want it to burn too much.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody:
Incredible! Super easy recipe and turns out great every time. Definitely do the "scrunch" method with the parchment, there no need to be cutting and pleating it. And make sure to let it rest in the fridge overnight and then bring it back up to almost room temp for serving. I topped it with a pumpkin caramel sauce for Thanksgiving and a cranberry orange compote for Christmas. Yum!

I have also split the mixture into 2 6-inch spring form pans and it was the perfect amount!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I read that adding blue cheese is a nice flavor addition.  Same for almond extract and/or vanilla. My question is how much do I add of any of these ingredients.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Has anyone added lemon zest or juice to make it lemon flavored? My wife's birthday is coming up and she loves lemon desserts but has also been requesting cheesecake, so I'm wondering if this could be adapted!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Yes, two tbs of lemon or orange zest work great!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: This is delicious! I followed the adjustments posted for a 9inch pan and added a splash of vanilla. The sides darkened like the top, but had more of a burnt flavor because of the parchment. I just used a knife to trim the edges after popping the springform off.  It was good the first day, but it was amazing after being in the fridge overnight. I highly recommend  making the day before.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: As someone who must eat Gluten free I’m always on the lookout for an easily modifiable recipe and this is it! I subbed KA one for one gf flour and it was an easy replacement. The cake is as advertised. I took this to a dinner party and everyone raved about it and I didn’t pre-test the recipe, I just went for it. In my oven, this was done after 55 minutes. I will definitely be making this again! Loved it

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Can this be frozen?  There's only 2 of us at home and eating the whole thing would be glorious but probably too much of a good thing to appreciate.  Freeze half for later?

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I appreciate the resize guide, but can you not just halve everything?

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Can this be made with all purpose eg Cup 4 Cup Gluten Free flour?  Or cornstarch?  Or almond flour?

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Can it be made with gluten free flour?

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: King Arthur Cup for Cup would work well, but gluten free mixes are not equal so I would stick with the King Arthur brand for best results.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: You can actually just leave the flour out completely and it'll turn out just fine!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: @Ann yes, I did this with Kind Arthur’s 1:1 yesterday and it was perfect

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Question:  has anyone tried this in a regular fixed-side pan?  I was wondering if one could used the parchment paper to pull it out of the pan.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Similarly, I have made this recipe 3 or 4 dozens of times using half portions with a tablespoon or two of excellent vermouth to replace some of the sugar. It has never not been wonderful! https://www.bonappetit.com/recipe/basque-burnt-cheesecake

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: This was pretty easy to make, is showy and delicious.  Somehow it tastes as if there is a hint of lemon. And we didn't wait til it was fully cooled.  The warmth was nice.
