author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Elizabeth K says to wet the parchment paper. Not necessary. Just take a fresh sheet of parchment paper, crumple it.  Very malleable.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: This is my trick too! I can't imaging having to dry it off.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Made this for the first time and it turned out great! Beautiful results with minimal effort.just want ton of that “serve at room temp” requires significant rest after baking- this isn’t a 2hr recipe, more like 4hr. Next time I will make in the morning to serve for the evening.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I made Basque cheesecake. It's the easiest and most delicious cheesecake I've ever made/eaten. I used multiple sheets of parchment and came out perfectly.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody:
To the person who asked whether you can use a regular (not springform) pan and lift the cake out using the parchment paper. I did and it worked well. Used one big piece of parchment and lifted the cake out after letting it cool for a bit. Easier with 2 people as the cake was very jiggly. 
Very easy and delicious. And I don’t even like cheesecake.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Very good! I wasn't going to buy a 5th bar of cream cheese. Used 4 (32oz) and squirted some sour cream to make up the difference. Added lemon zest as well

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I don't know if this problem was unique to me, but the batter leaked out of the lined springform pan within 5 minutes of being placed in the oven and made quite a mess. I'd suggest setting the pan on a rimmed baking sheet to catch the leaked batter.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: OMG!  I made it just last night, and after cooling, I put it in the refrigerator.  After breakfast I took a hefty slice for me  -- just for me -- only for me -- cutting into it while cold, and let it sit out to come to room temperature.  LAWD!! so good. so so good.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: @Riley Temple I LOL about just me and only me

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I forgot to add the heavy cream and it still turned out really good, so they're not joking about low effort and high reward. Great same day and next day.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody:
I made this for family Thanksgiving dinner yesterday (despite having done very little baking before) and it was a huge hit. I made it exactly as per the recipe except that I left out the parchment paper around the rim. It released perfectly from the springform pan anyway.
Super easy too!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Any way it can be a success in a regular Pyrex baking dish or casserole?

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Has anyone tried adding orange and/or lemon zest to the batter? Thinking that might add a nice zing…

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Be sure to stir the batter in the pan to remove any air bubbles. Otherwise, this cheesecake is heaven, especially when eaten at room temperature.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody:
Squeezed a lemon and added blueberries
OMG

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Made as directed with just a splash of vanilla as suggested in some of the notes - turned out spectacular.  My oven needed about an extra 10 minutes to get a nice burnt top, just keep an eye on it at the end.  Also, thanks to those that recommended wadding up the parchment paper prior to laying in the pan - worked great!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: This cheesecake was super easy to make and delicious!  I made it just as the recipe directs - I left it in the oven until it was a deep golden brown -not quite as deep as the photo.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Made this with a 9” springform pan and multiplied ingredients by .9. Came out perfectly with a bit longer time to get the top as browned as pictured. I normally hate cheesecake. This was fabulous— so light and flavorful. Cut it into 12ths. Can’t see how to cut those in half without destroying the slices. I’ll make it again when I can afford the calories.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Holy cow! I've eaten and made more cheesecakes than I can remember but this is on a whole nother level. Amazing taste, sweet but not overly so. Great texture (I did refrigerate it for 24 hours before eating). The best part, super easy to make. It lends itself to a lot of add-ons. Obviously, this is not a pretty cheesecake so all the fuss about the parchment paper, how to cut, crinkle, wet it, etc is really a nonstarter. What difference does the appearance of the side make? Just make it!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I like to drizzle chocolate sauce that gels over the top.    Melt dark chocolate and add cream and butter until it is still thick but pours over the cooled cheesecake.  Beautiful and delicious

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: An accurate digital scale (grams) for dry measurements is far more accurate than volume measurements.  For liquids, a cup is a cup.  Thanks for providing both measurements for those of us without a scale.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I tried it for the first time yesterday- came out great! One thing I would probably change/add for the next time: a bit of lemon juice and zest to break up the sweetness a bit.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I made this for Thanksgiving and it came out AMAZING! So good. I used Sally Ann's 8" pan conversion, Edith's trick to crumple the parchment into a ball first and Brooklyn Kid's suggestion to add a graham cracker crust. It was very tall in the pan so I gave it 10 more minutes. Truly gorgeous and perfect. Made my husband very happy. I am very thankful to NY Times Cooking and the baking community.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Another parchment paper tip. When you use a whole sheet with folds and creases in it, all sorts of delicious brown, crusty little scallops form which are delicious. Neatness doesn’t count, in this case. What a delicious and easy cake! It’s scary to see how brown it gets, but worth it to let it go all the way.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Do they now have an American-style cream cheese in Europe? When my aunt from Paris was in the US (ok, some 35 yrs ago), she loved my cheesecake (very similar to this recipe, but without heavy cream, instead sour cream or whole milk greek yogurt, and she said the there was no similar “cream” cheese in Paris. I suggested using ricotta, and actually started doing a mix of ricotta and cream cheese for my cheesecake. Not the same as this Basque Cheesecake, but absolutely delicious!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Believe it or not, Philadelphia brand can now be found in almost every supermarket in France, in both brick and tub forms. Used to use St Moret as an easy swap, reducing the salt a little.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Philadelphia cream cheese is to found also in Italy and in romania...

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Not only in France, but in Italy and Romania as well...

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Made this exactly as written. Just throw the parchment paper in there, left it overhanging the edges. Didn’t need much manipulation. This is one of the best cheesecakes I have ever baked and I have baked a lot of them. Refrigerated overnight,

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: How long to cook if using a 9inch pan?

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Thank you for the crumpled parchment paper tip- worked like a charm

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: It is not hard to make and came out well. I baked it at high altitude in Mexico City for around 60 minutes and it puffed up significantly above the pan, though it did fall and look perfect after cooling.  I suggest not refrigerating it; though a US version would perhaps be chilled, I prefer it at room temperature. I made a raspberry coulis that was a nice compliment.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Made this exactly to the recipe, and it turned out well. Make sure the ingredients are room temp and you'll get a light cheesecake.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Preheated the oven to 500 then dropped the heat to 425 and baked for 50 minutes. Perfection! Mousse-like. We actually ate it at room temperature because we couldn’t wait to dig in, and it was amazing. No need to make changes to the ingredients. My husband just finished a huge piece and is still swooning about it.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: This was amazingly easy to make & absolutely delicious!  I did ADD a scant tsp of vanilla bean paste for flavor & the little vanilla bean flecks.  Just before I poured the batter into my 9" and 6" spring form pans, I spread a thin layer of Almond Flour over the bottom of the grease pan, thinking it would help keep the cake from sticking to the bottom.  It made serving a breeze & added a beautiful golden layer to the bottom of each slice, complimenting the golden top.  So much better than grahams

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: What size spring form do you use?

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I followed the instructions and ended up with a delicious, but soupy final product. The outer couple of inches were perfect, but the center was liquid. The top had the beautiful brown color but would have burned with additional cooking time. Any suggestions?

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Check your oven temperature.  Mine runs a good 25 hotter than the setting.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I live in a particularly benighted part of the world, but even here in Wyoming, the internet has an oz to gram calculator. Recipes come out fine.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Too sweet, would use less sugar next time

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: This was a pretty easy assembly and bake. It tasted great! I left out flour since one of our household is gluten intolerant. It was not m8ssed.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I made this without a mixer, just by hand. I was concerned because there were little clumps of cream cheese throughout my batter. It came out ok though!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Instead of cream cheese, try farmers cheese for an even lighter texture. I’m pretty sure the Basques aren’t using Philadelphia cream cheese.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I did some googling and found this:   La Viña (where the Basque cheesecake was invented) uses the Philadelphia brand cream cheese and always has.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Really loved this one! I wanted to make the top extra dark so popped the grill setting of my oven on for the last 5 mins and it worked perfectly. I accompanied it with some simmered blueberries and it was delicious.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: 300 g of sugar

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: If I could go this 6 stars I would.  It. Is. So. Freaking. Good.  Not overly sweet, somewhat savory, smoky, eggy, creamy -- DREAMY!  Recipe indicates 12 servings but it can easily serve 18.  Or maybe even more, if at the end of a dinner party where everyone is already stuffed.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: It was so minimalist, I was skeptical, but it’s really delicious! Great texture!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: can this be made with Bob's 1:1 gluten free flour. Theoretically I would think so. Just wondered if anyone had tried.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: @sue yes! I did this and it was delicious. No one knew the difference

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody:
Any one can share how to adapt to Mexico City altitude, I had it over an hour in the oven and it still didn’t reach the 187f with the meat thermometer .
The oven temperature has to be above 400f I suppose .  Thanks for sharing .

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I made the whole recipe but used four 6" cake tins lined with parchment paper and it turned out beautifully. Same cook time and everything, although I started checking at 40 minutes and watched it like a hawk for the next twenty. I brought two of them to work and it was a major hit. Plus, because the tins were small enough that I just used one piece of parchment paper with rough pleats on the side for each tin I didn't have to worry about leaking.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Has anyone frozen this cheesecake successfully?

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody:
The recipe says to use a 10 in pan. My pan is more 9 in than 10. Would that work?
Also, can I use yogurt as a substitute for the cream cheese and heavy cream? Thank you.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Is this similar to Veniero's Italian cheesecake?

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: @ALICE SHERMAN SIMPSON Italian cheesecake is made with ricotta - so no.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody:
In metric system and German/Swiss ingredients:
~1 kg Doppelrahmfrischkäse (6 x 175 g)
500 ml Double creme de la Gruyère (Migros)
Ofen: 200 - 210 °C

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I just popped the cheesecake into the oven, so time will tell. I noticed the batter isn't very sweet at all, so I added a tad of vanilla paste to give it a little pizazz. I don't like to change up recipes until I've tried the original first. So, fingers crossed because this will be our New Year's Day celebration cake.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Loved this cake. Ridiculously easy and guests raved about how good it was.  I made the following adjustments: substituted in 10 oz goat cheese for 10 oz of cream cheese and added a tiny amount of vanilla extract.  The goat cheese gave it a bit of a “can’t quite put my finger on it but I like it” quality.  Highly recommend.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: This came out so well: beautiful and delicious! One issue I had was parchment stuck on the bottom. Are you all moving this to another dish to serve? I did when it was cool…wondering how to avoid for the future…

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: AP I let mine cool on the counter for a few hours before moving it to the refrigerator overnight. I think that helped with the paper not becoming so sticky all around.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Amazing!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Made this, it's as advertised: a great (and easier) take on a cheesecake. Might have left it in a half-minute too long, as the caramelized crust had a slightly burnt taste to it, but even that worked well with the custard's flavors.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: No need to adjust a thing! Have made this twice and both time followed the recipe to a t. Voila! A perfect cheesecake every time. It fits any occasion and is quite simple to make.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Made exactly as written and it was fantastic.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Oh my oh my oh my - this was uncommonly good. I 60%’d the recipe (so I could use 3 eggs), added some vanilla and goat cheese, and poured the batter in an 8” springform. Baked for about 60-65m and then in the fridge over night. UNBELIEVABLE.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Anybody make this fabulous cheesecake with 100% goat cheese?

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I substituted half of the cream cheese with nonfat yogurt. I hung it in a cheese cloth to dry it out a bit. Worked well, flavor is great, and now I can eat twice as much.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: 1% healthier, 100% less delicious

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: So easy and delicious!  I love this cheesecake because it's not too sweet.  I made macerated strawberries with balsamic vinegar and cracked black pepper for folks who wanted to add a little fruit to the top.  It was a big hit at Easter dinner.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I made this cheesecake last weekend following the sensibly resized measurements (Using 4 rather than 4 1/2 (!) packages of cream cheese) from an amusing and informative comment attached to the recipe (forget author’s name).  It worked perfectly, and was a great hit. Today I planned to make the cheesecake again. Unfortunately, I cannot now find the comment.  Clearly my bad for not making a copy, but it didn’t occur to me that the comment would be removed.  Am I overlooking it?

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Can I make this in an 8 inch pan ?

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: you may need to discard some of the batter.  it will overflow if you put it all in an 8" pan

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Help!  Have no springform pan!  2 9-in regular cake pans…. Suggestions?

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I’ve made this a few times, and today I accidentally forgot to add the cream. It still turned out quite well— tasty with a slightly firmer texture and a harder crust.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I grated a little lemon rind into the mix and it was a nice subtle addition!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Absolutely delicious. If I could give 6 stars I would!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: At la vinia in st. Sebastian Spain they serve it with a special sherry wine that takes it to another level! As I can’t find that sherry here I serve it with Marsala wine drizzled on top, which results in a similar awesomeness! Can’t be beat!!!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Everyone loved this! Next time I will add a few more minutes in the oven 65'ish, or so - for a darker top! So easy to come together too.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: This is phenomenal. I don't love cheesecake, but I love this. My family and the boyfriends and girlfriends did too. I think this will be on my regular rotation and it is easy to make.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Has anyone tried substituting cornstarch, GF flour or something else for the AP flour in this cheesecake? How did it go? I'm cooking this weekend for a GF friend

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Replying to my own note - I made it with GF flour and it came out without a problem! Delicious. I served with home made strawberry sauce. Friends loved it, and it presents like something much fancier than the amount of work involved

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Phenomenal and easy - a friend who has been a food broker for 40+ years said he's had every cheesecake out there, but this was by far the best he's ever tasted.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I find it's better cooked one day before.  The consistency improves overnight!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: This cheesecake was available in bakeries in Palma de Mallorca when I lived there in the 70s.  I loved it then and so glad, I finally have a recipe that replicates.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Flavor is better when salt is omitted- cream cheese already has enough salt.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Could you make this with GF flour? Or substitute almond flour?

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Could I add some pumpkin puree and spices to bring this cheesecake into alignment with Thanksgiving dinner - and still have a successful result?

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Fabulous! Definitely needed full hour in my oven, so keep checking after 45 minutes. Used 2Tblsp of lemon paste as well as vanilla; flavor delicate but still pretty bland, served with puréed, unsweetened fresh apricots to get a little bite!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Has anyone ever added pumpkin puree to this cheesecake? If so is there something I need to increase or decrease?

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: This recipe is super easy and delicious! I made it twice and it is so forgiving that it even turned out yummy when I forgot to add the flour the second time around! :)  The look and taste reminds me of the cheesecake from my German background, less sweet than cheesecake in a crust. It is dense, but you can lighten it up by serving it with berries.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: This was good as is, but I'm glad I made a topping for it. Did the cherries jubilee recipe and as a pair it was out of this world.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Made this for Thanksgiving and it came out fabulous. I baked it for 55 min exactly, weighed everything in grams and milliliters, and tried to pleat the parchment as evenly as possible after crumbling. I’ll look for comments about halving the recipe for a smaller group though.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Fantastic. Used cornstarch as some suggested and I couldn't tell the difference, and I'm not the gluten-free one. Makes a massive cake - definitely party quantity. Smittenkitchen has a loaf pan sized recipe that I'll try next time I have a hankering for cheesecake but no party to justify this huge concoction.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Has anyone made this in individual ramekins?  How does it change the timing?  Internal temp?

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody:
Recipe amounts for a 9 inch pan. 

1.4 cups granulated sugar (280 g)
1.8 pounds cream cheese (about 29 oz) or just use the 4 blocks of 8 oz each. It’s fine. 
0.2 teaspoon kosher salt
4 large eggs
1.6 cups heavy cream (384 mL)
0.2 cup all-purpose flour (24 g)
1 T vanilla

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: @gregs notes - I just made mine in a 9 inch pan using the regular recipe.  It briefly rose above the side of the pan but has settled below the rim. I’m interested to see how this one tastes and looks compared to the ones I’ve made in 10 inch pans.  I used a 9 inch pan to get a thicker cheesecake that would more closely resemble the thickness of the ones at La Viña in San Sebastián.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: @Jeff L.  With my adjusted recipe in a 9 inch pan, it truns out perfectly. When I did accordingly to original recipe in 9, it rose but exceeded the sides of the parchment paper and I lost batter. So either more parchment paper, same original recipe, use 9 inch and you’re fine OR “normal” parchment paper, adjusted recipe, and 9 inch. Either way, heaven.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Is it possible to make 1/2 the recipe for a smaller cheesecake? if so, any alterations to ingredient quantities or baking time?

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody:
@Mary B
Yes, I bake 1/3 of the recipe in a 6” springform. I bake it in my Breville toaster oven and usually notice that it’s done by the 35 minute mark. You’ll want to keep an eye on it near the end once it’s picked up a good amount of color. A dark brown color on top is good, don’t let it reach near-black.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: @Lola yes -- the recipe I used was from Katherine Lewin's big night but king Arthur and smitten kitchen both have versions that use pumpkin

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I made this for the first time for Thanksgiving.  I followed the recipe exactly and it was delicious.  However,  it took 75 minutes in my fan-assisted oven.  Don't freak out if it's taking longer to bake than expected.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Cooking time was longer.  At 60 minutes it was not brown on top, so I left it in and then it quickly puffed up and browned beautifully.  Very delicious, but best to make the day before you wish to serve.  Refrigerate overnight then let warm a bit, but we liked it a little colder than room temperature.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Changing recipe amts using feature works great!! However recipe times don’t change to accommodate, so user beware.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Anyone try this recipe using 1/4 cup almond flour or gf flour?

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: This recipe calls for a lot more flour than others on the web as well as fewer eggs.  You might want to check those out as I found one that calls for 1 Tablespoon of flour, which could also be omitted.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I’ve made this twice. It’s delicious and a hit. Has anyone figured out how to slice it and serve it without it crumbling apart and gumming up on the knife? I can’t get a clean cut.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: My grandmother always taught me to rinse the knife in cold water and wipe it clean with each cut and I seldom have this issue. Hope this helps.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: There are many different types of "cream cheese."  Some people call "cottage cheese" by the name "cream cheese,"  What works best.....mascarpone, Philadephia Cream cheese, Country cottage cheese......???

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: @ultimateliberal Philadelphia Cream Cheese

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: so yummy!  and easy to make.  Made in 9" springform pan, obtaining correct amounts by multiplying given measurements by 0.81

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Added about 1 vanilla bean's worth of vanilla, and reduced the sugar by about 10% and it turned our absolutely delicious!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I somehow forgot to add the cream - but refrigerated overnight and the next day it was a delicious burnt New York style cheesecake. Used the cream to make whipped cream and reduced a bag of frozen dark cherries with a bit of sugar and salt & almond extract on the stove for a cherry compote. Made our Valentine’s Day.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: add 2 tsp vanilla and the zest and juice from 2 lemons

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I used the recipe scaler to cut the recipe in half. Baked in a 9 x 5 loaf pan for 45 minutes and it came out great.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: The first time I made this as directed, and it was amazing. An easy and sophisticated dessert, delish. This time, I had a completely random amount of cream cheese and a little goat cheese in the fridge — we kept inadvertently opening but not finishing new packages for bagels in the morning. It came out to a little more than a pound, so I scaled the rest of the recipe down to match, mixed it all with an immersion blender, and poured the batter into a cupcake tin. For fun, I added some vanilla and lavender. Baked for around 30 minutes. It came out beautifully. So, all of that is to say, this recipe provides a wonderful basis for not following it at all.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody:
Made this and it was as easy and delicious as the article/recipe promised. 

One tip: Put it on a baking sheet to protect the bottom. 

The top took 1.25 hours at 400ºF to turn "toasted marshmallow brown". The cooked bottom was a very deep delicious brown too but would have burned in a few more minutes. Glad I used the baking sheet.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Appreciate the comments about wadding up the parchment paper before lining the pan - worked like a dream.  Made this as written without any additional flavoring (no vanilla, no lemon, etc).  Whole family loved it - even the ones who are not desert eaters.  Great recipe and very easy to make!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody:
"Mr. Rivera said, ---in 1997, at the suggestion of a chef visiting from a high-end hotel, he stopped refrigerating the cakes to give them a softer center. " 
So refrigeration actually voids the special quality of the original cheesecake recipe.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Has anyone tried this with gluten free flour?

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: To make this gluten-free, I can simply use my all purpose GF flour. But I also wonder: do you think I could use finely ground walnuts (or another nut?) instead?

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody:
This was the way I learned to make cheesecake long, long ago. I spent the next decade going through countless methods, recipes, and springform pans before coming up with a better way.

I found the perfect springform pan at a cooking supply place (and bought many of them) and still think the water-bath method is superior. I also don't like the absence of a crust.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: What is the kind of pan that you found and love?

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: This sounds absolutely fantastic… would it work as well with almond flour? I am gluten intolerant.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: this is the best cheesecake in the universe.  Hands down.  YUMMMMMMM

commentBody: I would think it should be refrigerated.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I’m going to try this with a direct conversion using all purpose GF flour… excited!!

commentBody:
Gus Nothing better than this delight with a portion of cooked peppered strawberries and a dollop of creme fraiche. Reply LindaN Cooked peppered strawberries...?!
I think Gus is referring (or may be not) to Strawberries Romanoff, which were peppered. I suppose you cold saute the peppered strawberries in butter and serve them with creme fraiche (or Chantilly)  along side the Burnt Cheesecake. Why not?

commentBody: I signed up for a class in Basque cooking when I first saw a pic of this amazing cheesecake in the ad.  It showed it still in the pan with the scorched parchment poking out. I thought it was a phyllo crust, was disappointed when I found out that it was crustless. When I make this I always use a phyllo crust.

commentBody: No cream cheese here in France near the Spanish border. What would be the European substitute?

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Neufchâtel cheese, Quark and mascarpone are a few alternatives I found with a quick google search.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Yes i've used it many times before with this recipe. I've been baking this for years.

commentBody: I just made this cheesecake. The flavor is lovely, but even after adding 15 min of time, the top did not brown darkly. And now as I unfold the parchment paper, there is a lovely golden brown syrup on the edge of the cake. Typical??

commentBody: I substitute some of the cream cheese with goat cheese. Works really well.

commentBody: I avoid sugar for health reasons. Yet this has appeal. I wonder if it could be made savory, with Jess sugar and perhaps a few herbs

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: the grease keeps the paper from moving around

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: followed the directions to a T, (cooked 55 min) came out perfect, think the middle oven tray is important, the 10 inch barely held it in but it did. so easy and all quests loved it. Served room temp next day. I did see some other recipes, basically the same but included vanilla, I might try that next time. Thanks I now have confidence in making a great cheese cake. We have our own organic eggs, think that also was part of success.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: For those using Celsius measurements, preheat your oven at 205 degrees Celsius. Thank you Sally Ann for the measures to fit in an 8-inch springform tin. Used this for grandson’s birthday cake, with the middle covered by fresh blueberries and halved strawberries, sprinkled with icing sugar. Nothing left over! Certainly a new family favourite.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: This is AMAZING! Only thing I stressed about was making sure it was browned enough on top. I will hit it with broil next time.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I made this for my daughters birthday this year. It turned out beautifully! perfectly delicious. I was worried that there was no crust, but I did not miss it when we actually ate the cheesecake.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: made to directions, worried about 10 in pan  barely a 1/2 in leeway, no problem with overflow. How is important is middle rack? kept at room temp day 1 and night of cooking, put in refrig on day 2 for about 8 hours, took out to serv room temp 8 hours later was fantastic. Served day two , back in refrig day 3, served day 4 again, it had only consolidated to the consistency of a traditional cheese cake from NY. easily served 10. so make day 1 and serve until day 4 in perfect shape. can't beat.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Unbelievably delicious.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Outstanding and pretty easy! I served it with a simple blueberry compote and it was the hit of the orchestra party!!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: best cheesecake i’ve ever eaten.  love the artisanal character, the caramelization.  super creamy, but lighter than many cheesecakes and loved the lack of crust.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Delicious and easy! Followed the recipe to the letter. Had to force myself to "burn" it as directed-this is really key to the unique flavor of this cheesecake. We'll be adding this to our permanent recipes!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody:
An amazing recipe.  I added 1/4 tsp. of vanilla and almond extract.   Delicious, easy..

For the person commenting on getting 24 pieces, the trick is to use a sharp knife - I use a serated bread knife - and to wipe the blade clean and wet it in warm water.  I cut 5 rows by 4, so I only get 20.  I’m willing to bet you COULD go 5 by 5, but I think you will be happier with 20.

It was delicious with berries, but I think next time I will serve it with a whipped Dalgona coffee sauce.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Turned out perfect for me. Everyone had two pieces and went to bed with good dreams.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Can you suggest which kind of cream cheese is best to use . Philadelphia ? Maybe whole milk ricotta ? Does it matter ?

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Philadelphia works well.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I tried this for the first time yesterday and the family loved it!  Very light and easy to make and the finished cake looked about as close to the photo on NYT as anything I’ve ever made.  I personally prefer the dense New York cheesecake to this, but this was very good and will stay in the recipe box.  Don’t hesitate to try it.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I’ve made this several times, just as the recipe states, with total success.  I do add a teaspoon of vanilla extract though - my only change. My pan is a metric size, just a little smaller than 10” but I just bake it a little longer to make sure the center is done and it’s perfect.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: This is my go-to dessert when I have parties... and no crust to fuss over! I can make it 1 - 2 days ahead, then serve at room temperature. Very easy to make and is conducive for young bakers since it provides a high level sense of accomplishment for relatively little effort.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I have to disagree with Michael. What's really confusing are the recipes with some ingredients for which quantities are given in the SI (International System, formerly the metric system) and others in Imperial System units. As far as I'm concerned, I translate every imperial measurement in the recipes I copy in SI units, putting the latter in brackets...

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I agree with you, although in this case I think it's a case of a lapse in concentration while writing the recipe. It clearly should read 2 1/4 pounds / 1Kg cream cheese. I wish the editors would make the effort to read the comments and correct such errors.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I have the same memories from the Basque region as Victoria Corcoran: in the Basque region the cake is served with a black cherries jam. In Montreal where I live, a French pastry shop in my neighbourhood sells a Basque cake with a lemon cream. Maybe not traditional, but delicious nevertheless...

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I don't understand why all the parchment paper when using a springform pan.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Cooked per the recipe. The tip on crumbling the parchment paper was very helpful. I let it cool completely and then rest overnight. It made removing the paper / out of the pan much easier. Delicious!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Bake this until its just dark brown, not black.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I'm waiting for mine to set and cool.  I used 4 different cream/rustic cheeses (mascarpone, American, European farmers' and Mexican cremosa) and heavy cream and beautiful eggs from pasture raised chickens.  I'm afraid I may have overbaked because I was waiting for it to be jiggly in the center, and not jiggly all over!  My instant read thermometer was way over 185.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody:
Divide all in half. 
No salt at all.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Delicious! I topped it off with thinly sliced strawberries and a drizzle of honey, just before serving.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: As per recipe but 300ml double cream.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: 300ml double cream

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I didn’t achieve the burnt look as hoped, even after upping the temp to 450 for 20 min

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: This is easy to put together but it doesn't look or taste like it was -- that's my favorite kind of dessert! Two things: although it is good practice in baking to always sift the flour / dry ingredients, I missed that in the directions here and simply poured the flour in after measuring. The result was some clumpiness that was difficult to stir out, and it also impacted the texture of the cake unfortunately. Second, I made this without a mixer and it is absolutely doable.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: So good.  I was skeptical that it would actually have a toasted marshmallow flavor but it sure did!   Some people liked it better very slightly warm but I preferred it cold the next day.  Great recipe.  Wish I had some luxardo cherries to eat with it!!!!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Not as good as the hype. Added a splash of vanilla and that did not elevate it. Served with sliced strawberries. Cannot imagine how to slice this into 24. 12 maybe.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: The first time I made this following the instructions I could not achieve that perfectly burnished top. The second time I cranked the oven up to 475° for the last 15 minutes and it did the trick.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: This was my first forage in making a  cheesecake and boy was it easy. It needed an extra10 minutes to brown ( didn’t burn the top). Served with berries at a family dinner. It was incredibly delicious.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: So good!  I too allowed it to chill in the fridge overnight (let it come to room temp first) and the texture was amazing! Definitely a keeper!

author: Tanya Sichynsky

Made for Easter in a 9” pan. Crowd included a couple GF guests. A huge hit! Used

280g flour

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Made very similar recipe from Americas Test Kitchen and turned out great - and looked just like the photoKey is to watch closely and use digital thermometer so it doesn’t overcook.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Hosted a party of 25 - the cake was on a sideboard for dessert. The cake went first!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Use less sugar

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Amazing. I made mine two days in advance for a dinner party. Made the recipe as is but added a little vanilla extract. Served it with a berry compote. Absolutely divine. A chef I follow on Instagram recently made one with Humboldt Fog cheese and I'm dying to try that!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: One of my very favorite dessert recipes

commentBody: Didn't have gingersnaps or graham crackers, so I looked for a crustless cheesecake:  this was GREAT & it was not difficult to have one sheet of parchment paper with creases, extending about 2 inches about the top of the 10" springform without having to trace the bottom. (Didn't try to evenly pleat the paper; it was creased in part and in part smooth.) Thought the dark top might have a burnt taste, but it didn't.   It was fine  without refrigeration on the first night: sat on a rack for 5 hrs.

So far the Claiborne recipe is best for me although I want to try that tall creamy one.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Very similar to a baked cheesecake made in Yorkshire using cheese curd. If you can't find them, it's simple to make curds at home:Take 2 pts full-fat, whole milk. Gently warm to 100 F/37 C, taking care not to boil the milk. Remove from the heat and add 3 tablespoons of lemon juice. Leave the milk to curdle, about 10 minutes, then check; the milk solids will have separated from the liquids. Carefully spoon the curds into a small sieve lined with a muslin cloth. Weigh and use in the recipe

It is fun to make and a treat to eat. Enjoy!

commentBody: This cake is consistently delicious and a hit. Even better on the second day out of the fridge.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I used an 8inch cake tin, no spring form, baking parchment was fine. I made 80% of the recipe and had enough for 6 individual muffin pan cheesecakes. Still, the 8in pan very nearly overflowed - the sides are only 2in tall, definitely recommend a taller pan! The result was still good and got a lot of compliments. Next time I’d add lemon.

author: Tanya Sichynsky

author: Tanya Sichynsky

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: This Basque cheesecake has become my hubby’s speciality and everyone loves him for it. He makes a strawberry coulis and drizzles it on top. The tartness of the coulis complements the sweetness of the cheesecake — perfection!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody:
I've made this three times. Here's what I've done for the best result:
I add an extra 1/2 cup sugar
2 tbsp vanilla bean paste
55 mins on the middle rack then broil while supervised if needed for the dark brown top
Let it come to room temp on the counter then loosely cover and fridge overnight. 
I've never had any leftovers. This is by far the easiest and most delicious dessert for hosting. I serve with homemade strawberry sauce or warmed caramelized ganache.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: 6" springform is perfect for a half batch.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Followed this recipe exactly, except added vanilla as well and it turned out perfect. I cooked mine 55 min and let it cool for several hours before serving and it was the perfect light texture. Thanks for an amazing, and easy recipe!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I agree with less sugar.  I added 240 grams and made it that way twice to wonderful success.  People commented they liked that it wasn’t sickly sweet.  Also I cooked an extra 10 min. I did have slight burning on the bottom but the cake was fully cooked and still came out smooth and delicious.  I did cool it for 4 hours on counter until it was completely room temp, then placed in refer for a full 24 hours. I would recommend adding a berry compote or berries for flare only.  The rest of the cake alone is outstanding.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I add some orange zest and two tablespoons of Gran Marnier.  A spoonful of a kumquat compote over the top.  A small glass of Pedro Ximenez 1827 - a sweet sherry - makes the pairing

I serve with mixed berry sauce. It is always a big hit —everyone likes it and those with dietary restrictions really appreciate a treat.

a tip for smoothness: make sure to run the f

commentBody: YUM!!! Well loved. I’ll try baking it less next time (did 60min since I wanted the top dark) for more of a creamy interior but it was still delicious. I also added 2tsp lemon juice and 2 tsp vanilla extract before adding the flour!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I dislike recipes that explicitly call for "inconvenient" ingredient amounts. This recipe, for example, specifies 2-1/4 pounds of cream cheese, which amounts to 4-1/2 8-ounce packages of cheese. So you have to buy 5 packages and are then stuck with a half package left over. I say, just use all 5 packages (2-1/2 pounds total); it won't make any significant difference in taste or texture IMO.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: America's Test Kitchen has a similar recipe, with less cream and more eggs. I don't particularly like the burning on top, so I cover it in foil before it's done to lessen that. It keeps well in the refrigerator and is much easier to slice. (On one occasion I forgot to add the flour, so it was quite soft but still delicious chilled.) I did add a teaspoon or so of vanilla extract when I made it recently; not much difference in taste. Good with some berries macerated in sugar.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Too rich for me. But I used to love my mother's Strawberry Cheese Pie - with graham cracker crust - only 4 oz. of cream cheese and 12 oz. of (bulk) cottage cheese and a cup of sour cream, 2 eggs, 1/2 cup sugar, 1 tbs. flour, 1 tsp. vanilla. Fruit topping. Healthier, lighter, and easy to make. I admit cheesecake is not my thing, though.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
