author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody:
Sublime! So delicious, so delicate, sooooo easy.

I made per recipe except I substituted corn starch for the flour to accommodate some GF guests and I added some vanilla.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I first read the article in NYT about the baker who invented this, then found this recipe.  I've baked many cheescakes, always the New York variety, and I had never heard of this version.  It's amazing!  I love it.  The lighter texture with the slightly bitter almost burnt crust is a winning combination.  And it was the easiest cheesecake I have ever made.  It came out looking exactly like the picture.  A new favorite.  PS.  I scrunched the parchment thoroughly and it behaved itself in the pan.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I made two changes to this recipe: one for good reason (subbed in a gf flour blend to cater to allergies) and one for a stupid reason (subbed half of the cream cheese for ricotta, because in my local supermarket they only sell tiny blocks of cream cheese and I didn't want to unwrap them all). Both these changes made no difference to a delicious outcome.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: This was delicious, creamy and just what one would expect in a cheesecake from a high end restaurant.  Everyone loved it.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Crunched up my parchment paper—that worked perfectly. Otherwise stuck to recipe.  Looked just like the photo.  Obscenely delicious.  Next time I will add some lemon zest.  Definitely making again.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I don't often make cheesecakes, but isn't this recipe easier than most, in addition to its other charms? I love it as is, but next time I'm going experiment with a little vanilla extract, or lemon zest and someone else here suggested.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Can someone please explain to me is it 2 1/4 pounds or is it 36 ounces?

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I followed it to the T, but it never got that really burned look.  I baked it for a full hour.  I would have let it go more but there was a big crack developing all around.  In any case we will find out tomorrow.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Try with a Reese's Peanut Butter Cup, really elevates the fireside s'more and adds in the saltiness of which they speak right there with the chocolate, you won't be disappointed.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I’m sure everyone has their own idea of the best way to roast a marshmallow. For me, it’s toasted over the last of the hot embers—no flame! It must be rotated ever so slowly to get a perfect, golden crust surrounding a just-melted interior. While I enjoy a good s’more occasionally, a perfectly roasted marshmallow is a treat all on its own.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: If you have not tried leftover Peeps instead of marshmallows. . . .  Roast the Peep until the sugar caramelizes.   A small child calls this his favorite "roast chicken"

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody:
An easy improvement to the basic graham cracker, that I credit to my English wife, is to use a Digestive biscuit (such as McVities). Get ones with either milk or dark chocolate coating on one side. In addition to having a more whole grain texture, they eliminate the issue of the chocolate bar not melting adequately.
If you are bored with s’mores or want something less sickly sweet try making Danish snobrød (translation “twist bread”.) Bread dough wrapped on a stick. It’s amazing.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I’m not burning my marshmallow. Golden and not charred please!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I make an inappropriate amount of s’mores per year for someone who lives in a walk up with no outdoor space. Whether it’s in a toaster oven, over a burner, or the embers of a dying campfire, the perfect s’more for me involves a smidge of tin foil - just a big enough square to gently toast the cracker and warm the chocolate off the direct flame while the marshmallow gets all roasty and toasty.  This makes a messy, melty creation that gets gobbled up before the plate even hits the table.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody:
It's heresy, but if all you're after is the sugar hit of a marshmallow heated well with melted chocolate, a microwave is the way to go. Just watch it puff up and open the door when it's ready. About 15-30 seconds, depending on your oven's power.

Tough to bring along on a camping trip, though.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: As much as I appreciate the patience and dedication of a golden mallow, I do in fact plunge my marshmallow into the fire until it catches on fire. I let it burn for a few seconds, blow it out, and immediately transfer it to the cracker. It's not burnt it's ... intentionally charred.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: While a campfire is ideal, a marshmallow toasted over the open flame of the gas stove in the kitchen (on the end of a fork) is a good hack. A yummy variation is graham cracker, Nutella, slices of banana and marshmallow.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Try S’mores made with the marshmallow between two chocolate digestives. Digestives are the UK equivalent of a graham cracker (available in many US supermarkets in the ‘European’ section) - So simple the chocolate comes with the cookie and melts very readily with the warm marshmallow.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: You had me right up until you suggested burning the marshmallow as a final step in the roasting process. Slow roasted over coals (not flames) to a golden brown with the center melted is the preference of the overwhelming majority of those at our campfires.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Best way to cook a s’more indoors is to roast your marshmallow in a air fryer. Comes out perfectly golden and melted

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: S’Mosts:  Forget the campfire! Forget the chocolate & crackers! Place a marshmallow (coconut covered are best) on a paper plate in a microwave.  At no more than 10 seconds, it will swell like a watermelon.  Open door immediately and inhale directly from the plate.  Sugar straight to the brain!  Aaaaahhh!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Soaking wooden skewers for a few minutes in water will make for perfectly safe and authentically rustic roasting.  Metal skewers I'd just worry would get too hot for tiny tot's fingers.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Dark chocolate is definitely the way I prefer my S'mores...so yummy!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody:
Iced oatmeal cookies (“Mothers” is the preferred brand) is a huge improvement over graham crackers.

A York Peppermint Patty is a nice upgrade from Hershey’s

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Don’t sleep on placing the graham cracker with the chocolate close to the heat source so that it slightly melts before adding your perfectly roasted marshmallow of choice.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody:
Feel compelled to leave a note here, though I rarely leave comments anywhere due to the general state of the internet.

Please note it’s NOT necessary to buy an extra piece of equipment to avoid getting bark in your mallow. Simply whittle the end of the stick a bit with a Swiss Army knife to clean it up and sharpen it. (Side note - Doing this also means you’re not buying extra things produced in a greenhouse gas-emitting factory somewhere you’ll only use a few times a year!)

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I'm not huge on the chocolate, I love just the marshmallow. I've also seen an idea for "lemon merengue" s'mores, with a swipe of lemon curd taking the place of the chocolate. It's amazing.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Trader Joe’s came out with a fancy-pants graham cracker last year that’s a single square, slightly thicker than the standard graham, I can say without hesitation that it’s phenomenal for s’mores; both traditional and totally revolutionary at the same time.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Disagree STRONGLY on need to char! Oil on skewer a good tip!!!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: After placing the chocolate on the graham cracker, I always place it near the fire, on a adjacent rock or maybe the edge of the grate, so that the chocolate warms slightly, but not too much, by the time I've roasted my marshmallow.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody:
It is criminal to light the marshmallow on fire. A good s’more has a golden-brown marshmallow, which has the necessary crisp without a tasting burnt.

Also, the recipe is missing instructions about how to melt the chocolate. Ideally, you put the chocolate on one of the graham cracker squares on a rock near the fire and let it slowly melt until the marshmallow is done. You’ll want to monitor the chocolate so it doesn’t get too runny—it should be melted but not so much that it goes everywhere.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody:
1. break your crackers to the desired size. 
2. spread a layer of peanut butter on the cracker. 
3. add your desired dimension of chocolate.
4. toast the marshmallow
5. proceed as for s'mores.  

alternately, eliminate the chocolate, and make the toasted version of the Fluffernutter.  :-)

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: My favorite way of making a s’more is to substitute the graham crackers and lousy chocolate bar with a Petit Écolier  (Little School Boy) Dark Chocolate cookie. The cookie stays way crisper than a graham cracker and the quality of the chocolate is wonderful. Pop a hot roasted marshmallow on top and it’s a little bite of heaven.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Jet-Puffed marshmallows are specifically designed for making s'mores and are flatter shaped.  Building upon the comment to use Reese's peanut butter cups, we like the dark chocolate, thin version to make s'mores.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody:
S’mores are the perfect desert. So simple and you
do minimal work to make them. I am a strong believer that the chocolate should never be melted! Texture is everything with the s’more and the crumbly graham cracker, and gooey marshmallow needs that little snap of chocolate for it to all make sense.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: If you have homemade marshmallows, those are the best to use.  they don't catch fire, they just caramelize so nicely.  Trader Joe's has the best graham crackers I've ever tasted.  I like to smear peanut butter on one graham cracker and chocolate spread on the other.  The heat from the marshmallow makes them really gooey.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Quite possibly the best recipe I’ve read here.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: 2nd the Reese’s recommendation. If anyone in your group needs to be gluten free Schar makes a really tasty GF graham cracker.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Use dark chocolate covered Petit Écolier biscuits with a marshmallow toasted to one's personal preference. Much less headache-inducingly sweet than the traditional composition.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: The key is slowly rotating the marshmallow over hot coals, not flames until the marshmallow is golden toasty brown. As a kid my grandfather made perfect marshmallows, and since he was Fanish we all thought that Danes were the best marshmallow roasters in the world.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: A layer of potato chips under the marshmallow elevates the smore - otherwise it’s too sweet for me. I also use dark chocolate. The standard milk chocolate bar in smores isn’t cocoa forward enough.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: The LU petite ecolier biscuits are the way to go. They are a shortbread cookie with a slab of chocolate attached. Two for one and also the chocolate doesn’t slide off once the hot marshmallow hits.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Just came back from camping in beautiful & buggy n Maine. S'mores were, of course, on the dessert menu. I tried TJ's graham crackers thanks to a suggestion from a nyt reader. They are indeed delicious & more graham crackery! And more sturdy holding up well to the rigours of shoving them into a kayak hold & later the ever important plating when u press gently the marshmallow into the chocolate & cracker, but I digress. Went w my beloved Hershey's & whatever marshmallow we found on shelf. Perfect.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Skip the addition of salt and swap out saltines for the graham crackers. We did this once out of necessity and will never go back!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I prefer two marshmallows with the chocolate in between them. That insures that the chocolate gets really melty. Also, I often use Reece’s peanut butter thins or Ghirardelli’s salted caramel squares as my chocolate. I recently had a s’more made with two LU Petit Écolier cookies, chocolate side in. That was a nice option. But they were made with the large squarish marshmallows, which I didn’t think toasted properly, for some reason. And I still prefer my chocolate in between.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: The S’moreo is life changing. Discovered around the fire on lovely evening when we sadly had no available graham crackers….twist open an Oreo and place your molten marshmallow between the chocolate cookies.  Delightful!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: This also works well with the girlscout shortbread cookies that are coated with a thin layer of chocolate on the top.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Using Alton Brown's homemade marshmallow recipe I add almond extract to flavor a batch. Ghirardelli chocolate chips on Cinnamon Graham Crackers and a toasted Almond Marshmallow is bliss!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Add raspberries or strawberries.  Delicious.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: The best kind of s’mores are those made with a group of giggling, happy Girl Scouts.  There may be some flaming marshmallows that end up in the fire or Hershey’s chocolate bars that get eaten before the marshmallows but I guarantee you’ll have lots of fun and a tasty treat.  BTW - make sure to use the Jet-Puffed variety of marshmallows.  They toast quickly and with lots of gooey flavor.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: We recently stayed at a mountain cabin near Rocky Mountain National Park. The cabin had a few fire pits, which unfortunately we could not use due to BEARS. Instead, we microwaved each s’more individually for ten seconds. Not the same, not crispy and smokey, but works in a pinch if needed.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Our favorite, which we only seem to ever be able to enjoy is while camping, is the Belgian Smore (see https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Cookbook:S%27more). The chewy licorice contrasts with the melted chocolate and marshmallow creating a whole new experience. Viva smores!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Nutella instead of chocolate. Digestive biscuits.  Golden brown and jiggly marshmallows. That's my take.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: This Girl Scout suggests swapping the chocolate with a Thin Mint Cookie!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Try Dark Chocolate Hummus instead of the chocolate bars. It give the s'mores a great taste and is easy to spread.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: @Shirley F — do you mean dark chocolate tahini? That sounds delicious!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Would have rated this 4.90 due to suggestion of saltines instead of graham crackers - or digestive whatever.  Saltines???  Otherwise spot on - including marshmellow method.  In a pinch during a snow storm - toast the marshmellows over your stove burner - even electric works - or a fondue burner/chafing dish burner.  A bit odd - but turns out great!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I always put the cracker   chocolate close enough to the fire so it gets extra gooey while I’m roasting

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I’ve always roasted one marshmallow at a time. While I roast the second one, the first one gets a head start on melting the chocolate. When adding the second marshmallow, turn the whole thing over (ie put it on the other side of chocolate).

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Love the idea of the lemon curd, will try that soon. We have a firepit and make s’mores frequently. Our best hacks so far: Trader Joe’s graham cracker SQUARES, paired with Ghirardelli squares. We buy the assortment bags so each “S’more Chef” can select the type they like - my personal favorite is the dark chocolate sea salt caramel. As the roasted marshmallow heats through the chocolate exterior of the Ghirardelli square, it releases the gooey lusciousness of the caramel.  #peaksmores

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: Too hard when you could just make it in the microwave

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: My family likes to use the Hershey's Symphony bar with toffee and almonds, good flaky salt, and I insist on the red box of Nabisco graham crackers- I don't other store or brand named graham crackers.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: If you want to elevate your picnic game, this is the hot dog recipe that will do it! And don't skip the recommendation to use mayonnaise on the hot dog buns as you grill them - it adds more depth to the overall taste.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: This works well with a vegetarian dog (like Field Roast's franks) and I doubt anyone could tell the difference.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: Oh my! Was not expecting such amazing things from this recipe. I am blown away! Taco meets hot dog. Substations I made based on what I had on hand: lemon juice instead of lime, parsley instead of cilantro, Serrano instead of jalapeño. Also put some mashed avocado on hot dog under salsa. 10/10 would make again

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: @Angela so change the whole recipe? ;-)

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: this was so easy and tasty. we made it with sausage - what we had on hand -and it was delicious. the pico adds freshness to a spring/summer classic.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: At the risk of offending all the appreciative commentators here, I say no (with good humor)!  I've had lots of nonsense on a hotdog, especially here in Chicago where pickles and tomatoes are bafflingly common.  Again, no! Onion, mustard, and relish only!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: @John that’s for bratwurst.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: This could easily be amped up to a full "Sonoran Dog" with a bacon-wrapped hot dog, with squirts of crema and mustard, as well as catsup.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody:
Wrong! Wrong! Wrong! This is an abomination. Here is the perfect hot dog:
1: Vienna all-beef hot dog.
2. Boiled, never grilled.
3. Toppings: yellow mustard, finely chopped onions, 2 slices of tomato wedges, 2 sport peppers, a kosher dill pickle spear, relish, and celery salt.
Warning: Don't ever put catsup on your hot dog. If you do, a comet will hit the Earth and destroy all of mankind.
P.S. Have you figured out that I live in Chicago?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody:
@Grzegorz
Thanks for giving us all a good chuckle. (But I always thought that comet would hit us only if we put catsup on steak!)

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody:
@Grzegorz
A comet? Are you sure it’s not an eggplant that’s going to eat Chicago?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody:
@Grzegorz sorry you forgot Mary Ann’s Poppy seed buns.
That’s why no other area can have a decent hot dog. It’s not possible without poppy seed buns and no other region can be bothered with that essential ingredient, . And in New England where I currently live, not only do they not have poppy seeds, they have the abomination of split top buns

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody:
The split hot dog method was legendary on coastal CT for decades and usually with Hummel hotdogs, with snappy casings or without.

Jimmies of Savin Rock in West Havem, CT was the vanguard, I believe, with hot dog and lobster / clam shacks following suit up and down the CT shoreline to this day.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody:
@Ned

I have a small size Jimmys t shirt in good shape.  Probably fit a kid.  I don’t remember ever wearing it, nor my son.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: There used to be a guy in Mexico City who made the best street hot dogs ever with grilled sausage, pico de gallo, melted American cheese and bacon. Needless to say, the pico goes on top of everything else.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: Against my better judgement and decades of enjoying barbecued hotdogs whole. I followed the instructions on butterflying and cooking to the letter. Never again, instead of juicy, the ‘dog was dry, a situation not fully remedied by the excellent pico.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: Would highly recommend adding another component to these hot dogs: Some slowly caramalized onions. Delicious

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: I was a bit put off by your email subject line that claimed this as "the only way to eat a hot dog." Here in Montréal we claim the local 'steamie' as the tops: steamed weiner, bun warmed briefly in the steam cabinet, chopped raw cabbage (lots), finely diced raw onion, a small bit of sweet pickle relish, a strip of yellow mustard, and a sprinkling of cayenne. Best ordered in pairs: "Deux alldress, extra choux," and add: "patates" (soft, mahogany-brown, double-fried Montréal french fries.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: @Walt, you just took me down memory lane right to the Montreal Pool Room.  I would kill for one — no, two of those hot dogs.  The absolute best.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody:
@Walt Palmer. 

Sounds fabulous! O, Canada!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: My husband wasn’t too sure about this recipe, but for the last 30 minutes, all he’s done is rave.  I served it with a potato salad from Ina Garten, and it was fabulous.  I followed it to the letter.  I can’t wait to do it again!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: Absolutely delicious, and I don't usually use any condiment but PLAIN YELLOW MUSTARD!  Its a new flavor world out there!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: Sorry, but I stand loyal to Chicago dogs.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: If you like this, expand to Sonoran dogs! Wrap dogs in bacon add little Mexican crema, some guacamole, Mexican cheese of some sort. Super soft bun a must!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: Mayo on hotdogs?  Ewww

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: @Edward No. Mayo on the outside of the hot dog buns to help them brown.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: Agree 100%. Actually, I say the same "Ewww" to mayo on anything. Butter is better, every time. .

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: @Ned Jimmy's!!! Man, THAT brings back memories. When I was a kid 65 years ago we lived in Hamden, and on summer days we'd go to the beach in West Haven, then go to the amusement park (with that great wooden roller coaster) and finish with a hot dog at Jimmy's. Thanks for making my day.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: @The Heartlandsadly, Jimmys was just sold last year after being here for 100 years. Unfortunately their menu remained stuck in time and they failed to keep up with modern tastes.  The building and propert are now for sale. $3 million, last I heard.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: A bit of celery salt would add a Chicago twist.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: This sounds good, but I prefer the classico (chili, cheese and onions, and/or simply mustard of choice) and I would never split a dog's natural casing, which is the vital element of any hot dog. No casing, no dog.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: Slicing the dogs lengthwise (Rochester style) is the only way to cook a dog. Once you lay them out flat in the bun, you put on the toppings, put on the top and the toppings won't fall out all over the place like when you leave them whole.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: Please, please do not ruin the recipe by adding mayonnaise to a hot dog.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: @John K. The mayo is on the *outside* of the bun to help it brown. And even that is ‘optional’ Don’t you guys read the whole recipe?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: I agree with John, and it doesn't matter if it is used for browning the bun. Butter would be a better option for that. And heavens no, mayo is not allowed to be used as a condiment on a hot dog, ever.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: @Tracey Culver I think it said the “cut side” which to me would be the inside.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: Brazilians co something similar, but also add stewed warm tomatoes AND… drumroll, shoestring picnic fries (yes those ones in a can!)

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody:
@monika vail
I had so many street-cart hot dogs in Rio that had mashed potatoes and sweet corn as toppings too!  Weirdly delish.  Apparently anything is good on a hot dog!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody:
@monika vail. 

The shoestrings sound fab ( also GREAT in a tuna mayo on soft white bread)but no warm tomatoes for me. I

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: Mayonnaise?!  On a hot dog!  Next someone will propose ketchup -- yuck!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: @Bob H. See reply to Edward, above.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: I love this on my hot dogs. Jalapeños are too much for me so I sub in minced Greek Mazzetta peperoncini. Always add Mayonnaise.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is nothing more than the ubiquitous "Chori-Pan" found at most weekend asados in Argentina. Except they are made with a form of a sausage...not a hot dog.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: making several hasselback cuts on a hot dog is easier to pull off than butterflying them, and you get so many more charred and crispy edges.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: I'll try that. I've got a hasselback slicer(?) ... what do you call those things? I guess it's kind of a jig.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: But then you can't open the hot dog like a book, which is the point.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: Surprisingly delicious and an easy way to elevate the adults’ hotdogs when making dinner for mixed ages. Will definitely do this again during summer grilling season.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: I note you have sidestepped the delicate issue of recommending a brand of dogs, which may be the most important part of the equation. Any thoughts?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: My thought: use your favorite brand.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody:
I agree, Bill. I grew up eating Oscar Mayer hot dogs, so I like them, and my current go-to is usually Ball Park franks. Have had Nathan's, (both sold cooked at dog stands and ballparks and bought them at the store a few times to grill at home. Decent, but not life-changingly great).
So yes, I would love to hear if there is a brand that is considered a superior dog by anyone!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: @Bill Wirecutter has recommendations. https://www.nytimes.com/wirecutter/reviews/best-hot-dogs/

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: Top with sriracha mayo and add some mango in the pico and it’s dynamite!!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: Add mashed avocado. Best yet.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: A nice change, and delicious!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: Sour cream instead of mayo also works great!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: The perfect “at the grocery store at 530 and need to figure out an easy dinner” recipe. Definitely making again.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: We did a bunch of yard work today and were a bit dehydrated and not up for a complicated cook. The later summer freshness of the pico x mayo’d buns made this the perfect salty, acidic, crunchy “recovery” lunch.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: Try roasting the pico de gallo.  Make a foil cup and cook with the hot dogs.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: That sounds amazing!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: During a trip to Mexico, we were served hot dogs with sour cream instead of mayonnaise. It's excellent. That's our way to go now.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody:
Just go full Mexico City style. You can find these at any street hot dog cart in the city. Pico, sweet relish, ketchup, mayo on the bun, you can bacon wrap the hot dog, but personally I think that just makes it unhealthier than it already is. Throw some picked jalapeños on top.
You’re gonna need something for the heartburn afterwards…

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: Please, no mayo on hot dogs.  I've been doing this with flour tortillas for many years.  Salsa fresca or similar is a great condiment.  I prefer the flour tortilla to the standard bun.  Properly rolled it contains the contents better.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: These are almost Sonoran hot dogs, but not quite. Southern Arizona wonder and a must have from street venders every time I visit Tucson.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: Blasphemy... The Chicago Dog is perfection...#1...every other dog is last.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: The NYT promo in my email said: "A ridiculously good hot dog recipe for your cookout."  I was thinking, "If you need a recipe for a freaking hot dog ..." But I must admit, this sounds pretty simple and tasty.  :-)

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: This is a great recipe but, as a Quebec resident, where hot dog joints out number gas stations, turn off that grill, steam both the hot dogs and the buns. If you think that is nuts, try it, then thank me.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: Reminiscent of the Chicago style hot dogs I ate after graduating high school in 1968--covered in onions and fresh chopped tomatoes with mustard and salt and pepper ($.38 with fries). Absolutely delishious! I'm planning to make this recipe for the 4th. Thank you for the memory.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: I stumbled on this idea at Trader Joe's here in Los Angeles, they were serving them as samples in the store using their own Pico. Making your own Pico is the best for sure, but there are some ready made that aren't bad. There's only two of us, so the next night we use the extra Pico for shrimp or fish tacos.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: Great idea!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody:
Ex New Yorker here 
Mustard, sauerkraut, dill relish.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: @Karen Oliver but what about that red onion sauce the street vendors always have?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: This appears (suspiciously) to be related to the bizarre creation that Chicago alleges is a hot dog. A cob salad piled on a frank. Never mind that it looks delicious. It's an abomination almost as egregious as their tomato-casserole pizza. Hard pass.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: Sounds good. But my go-to dog features chili (no beans), cheese (sharp shredded cheddar) and some crunchy, mayonnaise-based cole slaw. And a hot dog with some serious snap.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: Sauerkraut and brown mustard, keep the rest.   But it all matters little as at my age I indulge in maybe one twice a year where they are provided.  I know I need to bring my own kraut and mustard here in Las Vegas.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: Why are these so good?    I made exactly as directed and my friend and I had two each, when we're usually one-dog people.     This is a keeper.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: Who eats 1 hot dog at a picnic?  Get real.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: I said "Chucrut" as it is commonly known in Santiago, Chile, but I really should have mentioned it as it is known in English: Sauerkraut! That is one of the ingredients of the Chilean "Completo" Hot Dog. If you liked the Mexican Hot Dogs, you will love the Chilean ones! I do not get a commission. I simply rediscovered them in a recent visit to Chile.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: Just find a recipe for a good Chicago-style hot dog, someone should open a shop here!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: @Jim. Unfortunately it’s not possible without Mary Ann’s poppy seed buns.it’s not worth bothering without poppy seed buns. Such a simple thing, but you’d think no one knows what poppy seeds are

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: GUYS, calm down!!! The mayo is for the bun only before you toast it.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: Question: How did you keep the hotdogs from curling up while cooking?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: Insert some wooden skewers through them lengthwise before frying them.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: These hot dogs are seriously delicious and will become my go to. Splitting the dogs and grilling on the barbecue is an excellent method

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: Serranos instead of Jalapeños

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: Why? Random proclamations without explanation aren't helpful.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: Agree, but sometimes I've posted notes publicly that were meant for private consumption like a reminder for next time.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: This goes very well with home made cilantro mayonnaise - it very elegantly elevated the overall bite, especially if you add a little bit of lime to the mayo - just enough to bring all the tastes together

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: Very good. There was a lot of pico de gallo left over. From now on, cut those ingredients in half.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: Leftover pico de gallo is useful for so many things from eggs to potatoes to dips for chips and so much more. A batch of pico de gallo never wants for use in my kitchen.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: You should always have some tortilla chips on hand, just begging for that extra Pico!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: If there was pico left over, you didn't pile it on high enough!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: This is the only way to eat hot dogs! Made it several times and is always excellent! I had green and red bell pepper to bulk up the pico! Great, easy summer dinner!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: This is the only way I’ve eaten hot dogs since trying it the first time. Butterflied, mayoed, and picoed. I love it!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: I cannot wait to try this with chorizo.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: I love pico de gallo, but nah… I’m a Chicago girl and love my Chicago style veggie dogs, complete with French fries and a coke. Why mess with perfection? Anyway, everyone knows if you don’t have a poppy seeded bun you have no idea what a hot dog should taste like. So no one on the east coast knows what a hot dog should taste like. And furthermore, in MA frequently you can only get top split buns. What the heck are those? And why bother?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: always tomatoes :'). I'll substitute red peppers (the pointed ones, not the bell peppers). other than that, this sounds amazing. thank you.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: Pico de gallo is tasty but if you have access to really good tomatoes (a rarity unless you grow them) I prefer a piriñaca using bell pepper instead of jalapeño.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody:
This looks like a good recipe however you also have one for Chicago style hot dogs on file.  In Chicago it is not unusual to have half a salad on your dog.  No ketchup ever!
Readers should check out the NYT take on a Chicago dog.

author: Genevieve Ko

commentBody: Ketchup, mustard and chopped onions is God's gift to hot dog lovers!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: This Pico topping pretty much mimics my favorite Chicago-Style dog toppings: onions, tomatoes, spicy sport peppers, and a dash of celery salt (instead of cilantro).  They taste even better when watching the Cubs at Wrigley. :)

author: Genevieve Ko

commentBody: No mustard? It's not a hotdog without mustard. If you'd call this something else but not a hotdog, it sounds pretty good.

author: Genevieve Ko

commentBody: Hot dogs butterflied like this with grilled onions, grilled mushrooms and grilled peppers have been a favorite of mine for years.  Out of this world!!

author: Genevieve Ko

commentBody: Oh my God but in Mexico, Hot Dogs coming with something like pico de gallo but is not really pico de gallo because if you want to prepare Pico de Gallo is preparing with cilantro (coriander) That is nothing new to us.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody:
As a lover of Chicago Style Hot dogs, I could roll with the pico de Gallo approach, especially since I love pico de gallo and Chicago Style involves tomato, onion and pickle relish.  But somehow I will have to work back in the dill pickle, sport peppers and mustard and celery salt.
Mayo on hot dogs is beyond the pale for me!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: it's on the bun to toast...

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: Mayo for toasting the bun is a different thing than squirting mayo on the hot dog as a condiment just before eating it.  It's like making the best grilled cheese sandwich - Mayo on the outside before grilling makes it golden and delicious.

author: Genevieve Ko

commentBody: My hot dog is dressed in dijon mustard, and topped with diced onions and fresh jalapeños, no beans chili, and cheddar cheese

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: Ha! Make this frequently. Often flanked with slaw dogs and/or chili dogs. Adding some thinly sliced avocado is a winner too. The only problem I have yet to overcome is finding suitable buns.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: For extra kick, add chopped Kim here to list of toppings.  Also grill or broil hot dogs to increase snap.  Better still deep fry the hot dogs

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: Want to take this to another (delicious) place? Sub spicy kimchi for pico de gallo. We adapted this from Chef Roy Choi's Kogi Dog recipe out here in sunny Los Angeles (and if you can find Snake River Farms gourmet pups you won't regret it). Enjoy!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: Up your game to a true Sonoran Dog by wrapping the link in bacon before grilling.  If you can locate a Mexican bolillo, open the top for the "dogo." Topping with pico de gallo is a good start, but the real thing adds lines of yellow mustard, catsup, mayo, crema and a sprinkle of crumbled queso cotijo.  Sublime!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: @Charlie and pinto beans. I live in Tucson and the beans are a must

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: You have not experienced hot dog heaven until you have sampled a "Completo" (hot dog topped by chopped tomato, chucrut, mayo, or a "Completo Italiano" topped by chopped fresh tomatoes, fresh smashed avocados, and mayo, created in Chile. You can use turkey franks, and boil them in water with a couple of basil leaves. Toast the buns in the oven and assemble. You can watch a video on YouTube by Chilean Chef Alvaro Barrientos, entitled "Completo" here: https://youtu.be/RnlqaXCmrCA?si=4Z9QijGf21bFkks

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Hot Dogs With Pico de Gallo Recipe
commentBody: we are great Believers in the Costco hot dog. there are a few condiments at Costco to apply to said dog. when we lived in Mexico, one of the condiments was replaced with finally sliced jalapeno peppers. a simple hot dog indeed. perhaps we will try this alternative as I haven't eaten a grilled hot dog since my childhood. thanks for this eccentric "yuppie" dog recipe.
